q: An ML engineer needs to prepare customer data from S3 for a classification model. The data contains missing values, inconsistent date formats, and a highly imbalanced target variable. Which service should be used to handle these data quality issues at scale?
options: Amazon SageMaker Data Wrangler for visual data preparation and handling missing values
Amazon Athena for SQL-based data transformation
AWS Glue DataBrew for data profiling and standardization
AWS Lambda for custom Python data cleaning scripts
explain: SageMaker Data Wrangler provides visual interfaces for handling missing values, inconsistent formats, and class imbalance through built-in transformations.

q: An organization stores raw customer transaction data in S3 with Parquet format, containing millions of records. They need to extract features and split data into training/validation/test sets for an XGBoost model. What is the most efficient approach?
options: Use Spark on EC2 instances for manual data processing
Use Amazon Athena to query S3 data, then download locally for splitting
Use AWS Glue ETL jobs to transform data and write train/validation/test datasets to separate S3 paths
Use SageMaker Processing jobs with pandas to transform and split data
explain: AWS Glue ETL is optimized for scalable data transformations and can efficiently write split datasets directly to S3 for SageMaker training jobs.

q: An ML engineer is building a feature store for recommendation models. Multiple teams need access to pre-computed features like user purchase history and product ratings. Which AWS service should be used?
options: Redshift for storing historical features only
Amazon SageMaker Feature Store for centralized feature management and versioning
DynamoDB for real-time feature retrieval
RDS database for ACID compliance on features
explain: SageMaker Feature Store enables feature sharing, versioning, and both online/offline feature retrieval across teams and models.

q: A medical imaging dataset has severe class imbalance: 95% normal images, 5% disease images. An ML engineer needs to address this before training a CNN. Which technique is most appropriate?
options: Remove all normal images to balance the dataset
Oversample the minority class by simple duplication only
Use stratified k-fold cross-validation without any resampling
Use data augmentation on minority class and implement class weights in the loss function
explain: Data augmentation (rotation, flipping for images) combined with class weights handles imbalance effectively while preserving data diversity.

q: An organization uses SageMaker Ground Truth to label a dataset of 10,000 images for object detection. The labeling task is complex and requires expert annotators. Which ground truth task type should be used?
options: Bounding Box annotation with private workforce
Custom labeling interface without quality checks
Semantic Segmentation with crowd workers
Image Classification with auto-labeling
explain: Private workforce is recommended for complex object detection tasks requiring expertise, while public crowds suit simpler classification tasks.

q: An ML engineer needs to engineer features from raw clickstream data to predict user churn. The raw data includes timestamps, user IDs, and page visits. What is the first step in feature engineering?
options: Apply StandardScaler to all numeric features before feature creation
Immediately create a one-hot encoding of all categorical variables
Remove all timestamp information as it's not useful for models
Aggregate data by time windows, derive session-level statistics like session duration and page count
explain: Aggregating clickstream data into meaningful time windows first creates domain-relevant features like session duration and engagement metrics.

q: An organization's ML data pipeline reads CSV files from S3, cleans them, and writes cleaned data back to S3. The pipeline runs daily and processes 500GB of data. Which AWS service is most cost-effective for this ETL?
options: Amazon SageMaker Processing with on-demand instances
AWS Glue with Glue DPU pricing for flexible scaling
AWS Glue with provisioned capacity for predictable costs
EC2 cluster with Apache Spark
explain: AWS Glue DPU pricing scales with actual compute usage and offers better cost efficiency than fixed capacity for variable workloads.

q: An ML engineer is building a data lake on S3 with Parquet format for ML workloads. To optimize query performance for training data extraction, what should be done?
options: Partition data by lower-cardinality columns like region and date for efficient filtering
Store all data in a single unpartitioned table for simplicity
Partition data by a high-cardinality column like user ID for fine-grained access
Use Parquet's column-based compression only without partitioning
explain: Partitioning by date and region enables Athena/Spark to prune irrelevant data quickly, improving query performance on large datasets.

q: An ML engineer needs to validate that training data meets quality standards before submitting a training job. Data quality checks should include checking for missing values, outliers, and data drift. Which approach is best?
options: Implement automated data validation using SageMaker Model Monitor Data Quality baselines
Manually inspect sample data in a Jupyter notebook before training
Train the model first and assess quality through model performance metrics
Use only AWS Glue data catalog metadata for validation
explain: SageMaker Model Monitor's data quality baseline automatically detects missing values, outliers, and statistical drift against baseline constraints.

q: An organization has training data split into 80% training, 10% validation, 10% test sets. The ML engineer notices that validation accuracy is much higher than test accuracy, suggesting overfitting. What is the most likely cause?
options: The model is not complex enough
The validation set comes from a different time period than test set, causing data distribution shift
The validation set is too small
The validation data was accidentally used during training
explain: Data distribution shift between validation and test sets (e.g., different time periods) often reveals overfitting when test accuracy drops.

q: An ML engineer needs to train an XGBoost model on 100GB of CSV data stored in S3. The data has mixed numeric and categorical features. Which SageMaker training option is most appropriate?
options: Custom training container with manual XGBoost library installation
SageMaker built-in XGBoost algorithm with CSV input format
XGBoost from the AWS Marketplace
SageMaker Autopilot to automatically select the algorithm
explain: SageMaker's built-in XGBoost supports CSV format natively and handles distributed training on large datasets efficiently.

q: An ML engineer is training a BlazingText model for sentiment classification on labeled text data. The training job takes 3 hours. How can training time be reduced?
options: Use a larger batch size and multiple GPU instances with distribution strategy
Remove stop words from the text data
Decrease the embedding dimension only
Use a smaller learning rate to improve model accuracy
explain: BlazingText supports distributed training across multiple GPU instances, and larger batch sizes improve throughput and reduce wall-clock training time.

q: An organization trains multiple ML models in parallel for A/B testing experiments. SageMaker Experiments should be used to track which information?
options: Only the final model accuracy scores
Trial parameters, metrics, and artifacts like model weights for reproducibility and comparison
Customer feedback only
Cost of each training job only
explain: SageMaker Experiments tracks trial parameters, metrics across epochs, and artifacts, enabling comparison and reproducibility of model development.

q: An ML engineer needs to find optimal hyperparameters for an SVM model. The parameter space includes C (0.1 to 10), kernel (linear/rbf), and gamma (0.01 to 1). What is the most efficient approach?
options: Random sampling of 10 random combinations
SageMaker Hyperparameter Tuning with Bayesian optimization to efficiently explore the space
Manual trial-and-error based on past experience
Grid search trying all 200 combinations sequentially
explain: SageMaker Hyperparameter Tuning with Bayesian optimization intelligently explores parameter space, finding good values faster than grid or random search.

q: An ML engineer is building a custom training container for PyTorch on SageMaker. The training script uses GPU-intensive operations. What must be specified in the training job configuration?
options: Only the Docker image URI
The training algorithm from SageMaker console
A pre-trained model checkpoint
Instance type with GPU support (e.g., ml.p3.2xlarge), Docker image, and training script entry point
explain: GPU training requires specifying instance type with GPU, the custom Docker image, and entry point script in the training job config.

q: An ML engineer trains a deep learning model but observes that training loss decreases while validation loss increases. What does this indicate?
options: There is a bug in the loss function
The model is overfitting and may need regularization or early stopping
The learning rate is too low
The model is underfitting due to insufficient capacity
explain: Decreasing training loss with increasing validation loss is the classic overfitting pattern, indicating the model memorizes training data.

q: An organization wants to automatically build and test multiple ML models for a regression task without manual feature engineering. Which SageMaker service is designed for this?
options: SageMaker Clarify for bias detection
SageMaker Model Registry for model versioning
SageMaker Autopilot for automated model selection and hyperparameter tuning
SageMaker Pipelines for workflow orchestration
explain: SageMaker Autopilot automatically runs feature engineering, algorithm selection, and hyperparameter optimization for supervised learning tasks.

q: An ML engineer evaluates a binary classification model with the following confusion matrix: TP=80, FP=20, TN=850, FN=50. What is the model's precision?
explain: Precision = TP / (TP + FP) = 80 / (80 + 20) = 80 / 100 = 0.80. Wait, recalculating: 80/100 = 0.80. Actually the answer is 0.80 which is option A.

q: An ML engineer needs to reduce overfitting in a neural network trained on limited data (5,000 samples). Which technique is most effective?
options: Increase the number of training epochs
Use a larger batch size during training
Apply L1/L2 regularization and dropout to reduce model complexity
Increase the learning rate for faster convergence
explain: L1/L2 regularization and dropout directly reduce overfitting by penalizing complex weights and randomly disabling neurons during training.

q: An organization trains a text classification model using BlazingText on SageMaker. The model achieves 92% accuracy on the test set but 78% accuracy on real-world data. What is the most likely issue?
options: The learning rate was too high during training
The test set distribution differs from production data (data drift)
The model is underfitted
The embedding dimension is too small
explain: Large accuracy gap between test and production suggests the test set doesn't represent real-world distribution, indicating data drift.

q: An ML engineer is training a ResNet-50 CNN on images for object detection. Training on a single ml.p3.2xlarge GPU instance takes 8 hours. The engineer has 4 ml.p3.2xlarge instances available. What distributed training approach should be used?
options: SageMaker distributed data parallelism with batch size adjusted across 4 instances
Manually write custom training code with torch.nn.DataParallel
Train 4 separate models with different random seeds
SageMaker distributed model parallelism to split the model across GPUs
explain: Data parallelism is ideal for training large models on multiple GPUs; each GPU processes different data batches, scaling efficiently.

q: An ML engineer trains an XGBoost model with scale_pos_weight parameter to handle class imbalance in binary classification. What does this parameter do?
options: Applies feature normalization to numeric features
Applies a weight to positive class samples in the loss function to penalize misclassification of the minority class
Removes all negative class samples
Oversamples the minority class by duplication
explain: scale_pos_weight applies class weights in XGBoost's loss function, penalizing minority class errors more heavily to handle imbalance.

q: An organization builds a multi-class image classification model with 10 classes. The softmax loss function is used. During training, which issue might occur if class distribution is very imbalanced?
options: The learning rate becomes too large
The model may predict only majority classes, ignoring minority classes due to low sample representation
Underfitting across all classes
Overfitting on the majority class
explain: Imbalanced classes with standard loss cause models to predict only frequent classes since they dominate the loss gradient.

q: An ML engineer evaluates a regression model with predictions and actual values. The model shows high bias (systematic underprediction) and low variance. What should be done?
options: Increase regularization strength
Increase model complexity by adding more layers to reduce bias
Add more training data
Use a simpler model to improve variance
explain: High bias with low variance suggests underfitting; increasing model complexity adds capacity to capture true patterns and reduce bias.

q: An ML engineer needs to deploy a trained XGBoost model for real-time inference that serves credit scoring requests with <100ms latency. Which SageMaker deployment option is best?
options: SageMaker asynchronous endpoints with SNS notifications
SageMaker real-time endpoints with provisioned instance capacity
SageMaker batch transform for processing batches of requests
SageMaker serverless inference without capacity configuration
explain: Real-time endpoints with provisioned instances guarantee latency SLAs (<100ms) for synchronous requests with consistent throughput.

q: An organization deploys multiple trained models (V1, V2, V3) for the same task to SageMaker endpoints using A/B testing. 70% of traffic goes to V3 (new model), 30% to V1 (baseline). How is this configured?
options: Use SageMaker multi-model endpoints
Deploy separate endpoints and route traffic externally with a load balancer
Use production variant traffic weights in SageMaker endpoint configuration
Deploy all models in one container with routing logic
explain: SageMaker endpoint variants support traffic splitting; each variant receives requests proportional to specified weights for A/B testing.

q: An ML engineer needs to process 1 million images for inference with a trained deep learning model, storing results in S3. Latency is not critical. What is the most cost-effective approach?
options: Use SageMaker batch transform to process images in batches and write results to S3
Use SageMaker asynchronous endpoints
Deploy a real-time endpoint and call it for each image
Provision EC2 instances for inference
explain: Batch transform is optimized for large-scale offline inference, processing data in batches and writing directly to S3 cost-effectively.

q: An organization uses SageMaker Pipelines to automate the ML workflow: data preparation, model training, and deployment. A new dataset arrives and the pipeline should automatically trigger. What should be configured?
options: EventBridge rule to trigger pipeline on S3 object creation
Manual button click in SageMaker console
Scheduled cron job to run daily
Lambda function manually triggered by the data team
explain: EventBridge can automatically trigger SageMaker Pipelines when new data arrives in S3, enabling fully automated workflows.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model to SageMaker endpoint. After deployment, the engineer needs to gradually shift traffic from the old model to the new model (blue-green deployment). Which feature should be used?
options: Use SageMaker Pipelines for deployment
Deploy to a completely new endpoint and use DNS switching
Update endpoint configuration and instantly replace all traffic
Use production variants with shifting traffic weights over time
explain: Production variants allow gradual traffic shifting from old to new models, enabling safe blue-green deployment with rollback capability.

q: An organization trains models with MLflow in a SageMaker notebook. How should trained models be registered for deployment?
options: Upload models to GitHub for version control
Keep models only in MLflow server without SageMaker integration
Save model artifacts to local notebook storage
Register models in SageMaker Model Registry with metadata and version tags
explain: SageMaker Model Registry integrates with MLflow, enabling model registration, versioning, and approval workflows for production deployment.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model that sometimes serves requests very quickly (<10ms) and sometimes slowly (>1s). This causes inconsistent user experience. What could be the cause?
options: SageMaker endpoint instance is being auto-scaled and some requests hit cold starts
The model uses too many features
The data format is inconsistent
The model is underfitted
explain: SageMaker auto-scaling can cause cold starts when scaling up instances, resulting in slow requests until the instance is fully initialized.

q: An ML engineer needs to deploy the same trained model to multiple AWS regions for low-latency inference. What is the best approach?
options: Deploy to only the largest region and accept higher latency elsewhere
Manually upload model artifacts to each region's S3 bucket
Use SageMaker cross-region model package to copy the model and deploy endpoints in each region
Use a single global endpoint in us-east-1 for all regions
explain: SageMaker model packages enable copying models across regions and deploying regional endpoints for low-latency global inference.

q: An organization has sensitive ML models that should only be deployed in private VPCs without internet access. SageMaker endpoints are deployed in a VPC. What additional configuration is needed for model artifact access?
options: Disable encryption on model artifacts
Use VPC endpoints for S3 to enable private connectivity without internet gateway
Use public S3 bucket accessible over the internet
Place S3 bucket in the same VPC as the endpoint
explain: VPC endpoints for S3 enable private, secure connectivity without requiring internet gateway, meeting security requirements for restricted deployments.

q: An ML engineer publishes a model to the AWS Marketplace. An external customer wants to use the model. Which deployment option should the customer use?
options: Only view the model description without deploying
Deploy the model to EC2 instead of SageMaker
Subscribe to the Marketplace model and deploy using SageMaker console or SDK
Manually download the model and train it themselves
explain: Marketplace models can be subscribed and deployed directly to SageMaker endpoints, simplifying access for customers.

q: An organization uses SageMaker Edge to deploy ML models to IoT devices in manufacturing plants. Model updates are released monthly. How should updates be managed?
options: Manually update each device individually
Deploy only to devices in one location
Avoid updating models to prevent production disruption
Use SageMaker Edge manager for fleet management and automated model updates
explain: SageMaker Edge Manager enables remote fleet management, automated model updates, and monitoring across IoT devices at scale.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model and needs to monitor it for performance degradation. The model was trained on 2022 data, but production data from 2024 has shifted significantly. How should this be detected?
options: Wait for customer complaints about poor predictions
Use SageMaker Model Monitor with data quality baselines to detect statistical drift
Retrain the model immediately without monitoring
Manually check model accuracy daily by running test data
explain: SageMaker Model Monitor's data quality baseline detects distribution shift (drift) between production data and training baseline automatically.

q: An ML model for loan approval shows bias: 70% approval for male applicants but 40% approval for female applicants with same creditworthiness. How should bias be detected and analyzed?
options: Ignore the disparity as a natural outcome of the model
Manually review individual predictions without systematic analysis
Use SageMaker Clarify to generate feature importance and bias reports across protected attributes
Remove the protected attribute (gender) from the model to hide the bias
explain: SageMaker Clarify provides bias reports and feature importance analysis, quantifying disparities across protected attributes systematically.

q: An ML model is deployed to production, but the team needs to understand which features the model relies on for predictions. This is critical for model interpretability and trust. Which service should be used?
options: Re-train with different features manually
Ask the ML engineer for informal explanation
SageMaker Clarify for SHAP/LIME explanations and feature importance
CloudWatch logs only
explain: SageMaker Clarify provides model explanations using SHAP/LIME, showing feature contributions to individual predictions for interpretability.

q: An ML model for fraud detection has been deployed for 6 months. The ML team notices that model accuracy has decreased from 95% to 87%. What is the most likely cause?
options: The input data format changed
The servers are running out of disk space
The model is experiencing concept drift; fraud patterns have evolved since training on 2023 data
Too many predictions are being made
explain: Concept drift occurs when the relationship between features and target changes over time; fraud patterns evolve, reducing model accuracy.

q: An organization runs an ML-based recommendation system 24/7. They need to monitor cost, usage, and identify opportunities to optimize spending. What CloudWatch metrics are most relevant?
options: Customer satisfaction surveys
Endpoint invocation count, model latency, and instance utilization to optimize capacity
Only error rates
Application performance only
explain: Monitoring invocation count, latency, and utilization enables identifying over-provisioned capacity and optimizing costs on ML endpoints.

q: An ML model for medical diagnosis is deployed. To ensure responsible AI, the team needs to validate model fairness across different patient demographics. What approach should be taken?
options: Only test on the majority demographic
Use SageMaker Clarify to measure bias metrics and fairness across demographics in evaluation set
Remove demographic information from inputs
Assume the model is fair if accuracy is high
explain: SageMaker Clarify measures fairness metrics like disparate impact across demographics, ensuring equitable performance for responsible AI.

q: A deployed ML model serves 1 million requests daily. The team needs a dashboard to monitor real-time prediction latency, error rates, and data drift. Which service should be used?
options: No monitoring to reduce overhead
CloudWatch Logs only for manual log analysis
Email reports sent manually weekly
CloudWatch Dashboards with metrics from SageMaker endpoint and Model Monitor
explain: CloudWatch Dashboards visualize endpoint metrics (latency, errors) and Model Monitor drift metrics in real-time for operational visibility.

q: An organization uses SageMaker Model Monitor to track model quality. An alert is triggered because prediction latency has increased from 50ms to 200ms. What could be the cause?
options: Instance capacity is insufficient, causing queuing; consider scaling up or using faster instance types
The model has fewer features
Predictions are more confident
The model became more accurate
explain: Latency increase suggests resource contention or insufficient capacity; scaling endpoints or using GPU instances can reduce latency.

q: An ML engineer processes raw event logs from an application for churn prediction. The data includes timestamps, user_id, event_type, and duration. What feature engineering approach is most appropriate?
options: One-hot encode user_id directly
Aggregate events into time windows to create features like event count, average duration, and diversity per user
Remove timestamps as they can't be modeled
Use raw event timestamps without aggregation
explain: Aggregating raw events into meaningful windows creates predictive features like user activity level and engagement patterns.

q: An organization's training dataset has 1 million records. When split 80/10/10 for training/validation/test, the validation and test sets have only 100,000 samples each. Will this impact model evaluation?
options: No impact; test set size is sufficient
Test set may be too small for reliable evaluation of minority classes or rare scenarios; consider stratified splits
Validation set should be larger than training set
All data should be used for training only
explain: Small test sets reduce statistical power for evaluation; stratified splits ensure minority classes are represented proportionally.

q: An ML engineer ingests real-time streaming data from IoT sensors into a data lake on S3 using Kinesis Firehose. The data needs to be enriched with historical context before training. Which approach is best?
options: Manually join data in Excel
Use AWS Glue to join streaming data with historical reference tables before writing to S3
Ignore historical context
Store all data in DynamoDB for real-time access
explain: AWS Glue can enrich streaming data by joining with reference tables, creating enriched datasets for training.

q: An organization trains models on customer data sensitive to GDPR. The team needs to remove personally identifiable information (PII) before sharing training data with external partners. Which service helps?
options: SageMaker Data Wrangler for PII detection and masking
Manual inspection by data stewards only
AWS Glue DataBrew for data profiling and PII redaction
Amazon Macie for PII discovery in S3
explain: AWS Glue DataBrew provides built-in recipes for PII redaction (email masking, SSN hashing) to comply with GDPR and privacy policies.

q: An ML engineer's training data is split into 80% training and 20% validation, but no separate test set is held out. The model achieves 95% validation accuracy. What is a concern?
options: Validation accuracy is always representative of test accuracy
The model is underfitted
The model may show inflated performance; test performance could be lower due to overfitting on the validation set
More data is needed
explain: Using validation set for hyperparameter tuning causes optimistic bias; a held-out test set provides unbiased performance estimation.

q: An organization has images of products in different formats (JPEG, PNG, BMP) with varying resolutions (640x480, 1024x768, etc.). Before training a CNN, what preprocessing is needed?
options: Use images as-is without standardization
Remove images with different resolutions
Resize only JPEG images
Convert all images to consistent format and resolution (e.g., 256x256 PNG), normalize pixel values
explain: CNN training requires consistent input shapes; standardizing format, resolution, and pixel normalization improves convergence and model quality.

q: An ML engineer uses SageMaker Ground Truth to label customer support tickets for multi-label classification (multiple tags per ticket). How should annotation quality be ensured?
options: Only use expert annotators, excluding crowd workers
No quality checks needed
Use a single low-cost annotator per ticket
Use multiple annotators per ticket and measure inter-annotator agreement for quality control
explain: Ground Truth supports multi-annotator consensus; inter-annotator agreement metrics validate labeling quality before model training.

q: An organization's training data comes from two sources with different quality levels: database A (clean, validated data) and database B (noisy IoT sensor data). How should this be handled?
options: Use only database B for more data
Randomly remove 50% of database B
Combine both sources and apply quality weighting; assign lower weights to noisy database B samples
Use only database A to maintain quality
explain: Sample weighting can down-weight noisy samples, allowing both data sources to contribute while maintaining overall quality.

q: An ML engineer trains a neural network with a learning rate of 0.1, but training loss oscillates wildly. What adjustment should be made?
options: Increase learning rate to 0.5
Increase batch size to 1
Remove regularization
Decrease learning rate to 0.01 to stabilize training
explain: High learning rate causes gradient explosion; decreasing it stabilizes training by taking smaller steps in the loss landscape.

q: An ML engineer builds a model for demand forecasting using time series data. The data shows clear seasonality (weekly and yearly patterns). Which algorithm is most appropriate?
options: Random forest without time encoding
ARIMA or LSTM to capture temporal dependencies and seasonality
Simple linear regression ignoring temporal patterns
Naive baseline that predicts previous value
explain: ARIMA/LSTM explicitly model temporal dependencies and seasonality in time series, outperforming models that ignore temporal structure.

q: An ML engineer trains a model that achieves 99% accuracy on training data and 51% accuracy on test data. What issue is this model experiencing?
options: Severe overfitting; the model has memorized training data rather than learning generalizable patterns
Underfitting due to model simplicity
The learning rate is too low
The test set is too difficult
explain: Massive gap between training and test accuracy indicates severe overfitting; regularization, data augmentation, or early stopping are needed.

q: An ML engineer evaluates two models: Model A has mean squared error (MSE) = 100, Model B has MSE = 50. Which statement is true?
options: Model B is better because it has lower MSE, indicating closer predictions to ground truth
MSE cannot be compared between models
They are equally good
Model A is better because it has a higher error
explain: Lower MSE indicates better prediction accuracy; Model B with MSE=50 makes predictions closer to true values than Model A.

q: An ML engineer trains multiple models for classification: Model A (accuracy=85%, precision=90%), Model B (accuracy=90%, precision=70%). How should the choice depend on the use case?
options: Accuracy alone determines the best model
Always choose Model B because accuracy is higher
For fraud detection (false positives costly), choose Model A with high precision; for disease screening (false negatives costly), choose Model B
Choose randomly
explain: Precision vs. recall tradeoffs matter; high precision is critical when false positives are expensive (fraud), high recall when false negatives are dangerous (disease).

q: An ML engineer uses cross-validation with k=5 folds to evaluate a model. Why is this better than a single train/test split?
options: Cross-validation is faster
Cross-validation is easier to implement
k-fold cross-validation uses multiple folds to get more stable performance estimates and better utilize limited data
Cross-validation always produces higher accuracy
explain: k-fold cross-validation reduces variance in performance estimates by averaging results across multiple folds, providing more reliable evaluation.

q: An organization trains a random forest model with 100 trees. The model shows high bias and low variance on validation data. How can performance be improved?
options: Reduce tree depth and simplify the model further to reduce bias
Decrease the number of trees to 10 to simplify the model
Increase tree depth to allow trees to capture more complex patterns and reduce bias
Increase the number of trees to 1000
explain: High bias indicates underfitting; increasing tree depth allows each tree to capture more complex patterns, reducing bias and improving accuracy.

q: An ML engineer uses a learning rate schedule that starts at 0.1 and decreases over time. How does this improve training?
options: Constant learning rate converges faster
Large initial learning rate finds decent solutions quickly, smaller later rate fine-tunes near optimal point
Lower learning rate is always better throughout training
Learning rate schedule has no effect on convergence
explain: Learning rate schedules balance exploration (large initial rate) and exploitation (small later rate), improving both convergence speed and final accuracy.

q: An ML engineer trains a transformer model for natural language processing. During training, the engineer observes that loss decreases for the first 5 epochs but then plateaus. What should be done?
options: Increase the learning rate
Double the batch size
Continue training for 100 epochs to ensure convergence
Use early stopping to halt training and prevent overfitting when validation loss stops improving
explain: Early stopping halts training when validation performance plateaus, preventing overfitting and saving computational resources.

q: An ML engineer compares two models for a regression task: Model A (root mean squared error = 15) and Model B (RMSE = 20). Both are trained on the same data. Why might Model B still be preferred in production?
options: Model B is always better
Model A is always better due to lower RMSE
RMSE is the only metric that matters
Model B might be preferred if it's simpler, faster to train, or requires less computational resources for inference
explain: Model selection involves tradeoffs; simpler, faster models with slightly higher error may be preferred for real-time, cost-sensitive applications.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model endpoint on SageMaker. The endpoint is initially configured with 1 instance (ml.m5.xlarge). Traffic increases 10x. How can auto-scaling be configured?
options: Define a scaling policy based on SageMaker endpoint metric (e.g., CPUUtilization > 70%) to automatically add instances
Use only 1 instance regardless of traffic
Manually add more instances every time traffic increases
Switch to a larger instance type only once
explain: SageMaker auto-scaling policies automatically add/remove instances based on metrics, ensuring consistent performance under variable load.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model in production but needs to validate it before fully releasing. Which deployment strategy allows traffic to gradually shift from old to new model?
options: Blue-green with instant cutover
Canary deployment: send 5% traffic to new model, monitor for errors, then gradually shift if healthy
All-at-once replacement
Deploy to offline environment only
explain: Canary deployments minimize risk by serving small traffic percentage to new model first, monitoring for issues before full rollout.

q: An organization uses SageMaker Pipelines to automate model training, evaluation, and deployment. The pipeline should automatically stop and not deploy if model accuracy is below 90%. How is this implemented?
options: Run the entire pipeline without checks
Always deploy regardless of accuracy
Use conditional steps in the pipeline: evaluate model, then conditionally deploy only if accuracy >= 90%
Manually check accuracy and decide to deploy
explain: SageMaker Pipelines support conditional steps; pipeline execution can branch based on metric thresholds, preventing poor model deployment.

q: An ML team uses MLflow for experiment tracking and model management. The team wants to integrate MLflow models with SageMaker for deployment. What is the recommended approach?
options: Use separate model repositories for MLflow and SageMaker
Use SageMaker's MLflow integration to register models from MLflow Model Registry and deploy to endpoints
Keep models only in MLflow without deploying
Manually download MLflow model and rewrite for SageMaker
explain: SageMaker integrates with MLflow Model Registry, allowing seamless deployment of MLflow models to SageMaker endpoints.

q: An ML engineer deploys a recommendation model as an async endpoint. Users submit requests that are processed asynchronously with results written to S3. How are clients notified of results?
options: SageMaker can publish results to SNS/SQS for event-driven notification; clients can subscribe for updates
Clients must poll S3 repeatedly for results
Use only real-time endpoints for notifications
Clients cannot be notified
explain: SageMaker async endpoints can publish results to SNS/SQS, enabling event-driven workflows where clients are notified when results are ready.

q: An organization has models deployed on multiple SageMaker endpoints (endpoint A, B, C). A new deployment requires updating all endpoints with the latest model version. How can this be automated?
options: Manually update each endpoint one by one
Deploy models to EC2 instead
Use only one endpoint
Use AWS Lambda function triggered on model registry updates to automatically update all endpoints
explain: Lambda can be triggered by model registry updates, automatically deploying new versions to all endpoints, ensuring consistency.

q: An ML engineer needs to deploy a large language model (LLM) for inference. The model size exceeds available instance memory. What should be done?
options: Use SageMaker multi-model endpoints to load models on-demand
Deploy only during off-peak hours
Use a smaller model
Use tensor parallelism or model parallelism across multiple GPUs/instances
explain: Model parallelism splits large models across multiple instances/GPUs, enabling deployment of models larger than single instance memory.

q: An ML engineer wants to perform real-time inference on streaming data from Kinesis. Models are deployed on SageMaker endpoints. How should inference be integrated with the data pipeline?
options: Deploy models directly to Kinesis
Export data to S3 and use batch transform
Use Lambda to pull from Kinesis and invoke SageMaker endpoint for each record
Only use offline inference
explain: Lambda can be triggered by Kinesis records, invoke SageMaker endpoints for real-time inference, and write results back to Kinesis/S3.

q: A deployed ML model for credit scoring shows consistent high accuracy (95%) but the approval rate has increased from 40% to 60% over 6 months. What could explain this?
options: Model predictions may be drifting; underlying patterns have changed, or threshold was shifted without documentation
The model is working perfectly
More people are applying for loans
Interest rates changed
explain: Concept drift changes model behavior without accuracy change; drift detection should monitor prediction distribution, not just accuracy.

q: An ML team monitors a deployed model using CloudWatch and SageMaker Model Monitor. An alert is triggered for data drift. What should be the first response?
options: Investigate the drift: check if input distribution changed, if data quality degraded, or if new data patterns appeared
Ignore the alert
Immediately retrain the model
Disable the model
explain: Data drift alerts require investigation; drifted data may represent new real-world patterns worth learning, not always warranting immediate retraining.

q: An organization uses SageMaker Clarify to analyze feature importance for a credit model. The analysis shows that 'age' is the most important feature. However, using age for lending decisions is illegal in some jurisdictions. What should be done?
options: Continue using age as the most important feature
Remove age from the model or implement fairness constraints to ensure decisions are not primarily based on protected attributes
Only use age feature in some countries
Rename age to avoid legal issues
explain: Protected attributes (age, race, gender) should not be primary decision factors; fairness constraints or feature removal ensures regulatory compliance.

q: A fraud detection model deployed 6 months ago had an F1-score of 0.85. Current evaluation shows F1-score of 0.72. What is the first step in troubleshooting?
options: Increase the decision threshold
Assume model is broken and deploy a backup
Check for concept drift, data drift, or label quality degradation in recent data
Immediately retrain on all available data
explain: Performance degradation requires investigation; concept/data drift or label quality issues are common causes in production systems.

q: An ML engineer is responsible for model compliance and governance. A regulatory audit requires proof that model decisions are explainable. How should explainability be implemented?
options: Model explainability is not possible
Use only simple linear models that are inherently interpretable
Use SageMaker Clarify for automated SHAP/LIME explanations with feature importance and prediction attribution
Manual case-by-case explanations by data scientists
explain: SageMaker Clarify provides automated, scalable explanations for audit trails, meeting regulatory requirements for model transparency.

q: An organization's ML pipeline ingests data from multiple sources: database (SQL), data lake (S3), and API (REST). Data has different schemas and update frequencies. What is the best approach to prepare training data?
options: Manually combine data in a spreadsheet
Write custom Python scripts for each data source
Use AWS Glue catalog for metadata management and Glue ETL jobs to integrate and standardize schemas
Use only the database, ignore other sources
explain: AWS Glue provides centralized schema management and ETL, handling multiple data sources and standardizing schemas at scale.

q: An ML engineer is preparing training data for a computer vision model. The data includes images from different cameras with different lighting conditions, image sizes, and compression levels. What preprocessing is critical?
options: No preprocessing needed; use images as-is
Convert all images to grayscale only
Use only images from one camera
Standardize image size, color space; apply histogram equalization or normalization to handle lighting variation
explain: Image preprocessing standardizes size/color and handles lighting variation, improving model robustness and convergence across diverse image sources.

q: An organization trains models on sensitive healthcare data. Privacy regulations require that training data cannot be exposed to external vendors or cloud storage unencrypted. How should data be prepared?
options: Only process data on on-premises servers
Remove sensitive information entirely
Upload raw data to S3 without encryption
Use SageMaker Processing with VPC mode, encrypt data at rest/in-transit, and use IAM roles for access control
explain: SageMaker Processing in VPC with encryption ensures healthcare data privacy while enabling cloud-based ML development securely.

q: An ML engineer applies one-hot encoding to a categorical feature with 1000 unique values. This creates 1000 new binary columns. What issue could arise?
options: Curse of dimensionality: sparse, high-dimensional input increases overfitting and computational cost
Faster training due to more features
No issues; more features are always better
Improved model accuracy due to more features
explain: One-hot encoding high-cardinality features creates sparse, high-dimensional data, increasing overfitting risk and computational overhead.

q: An ML engineer creates a feature from timestamps: extracting year, month, day, day-of-week, hour, minute. This feature set is then used in a linear model. What could improve performance?
options: Recognize temporal cyclicity (e.g., day-of-week is cyclical 1-7); use sin/cos encoding to preserve distance
Add more granular features (second, millisecond)
Remove temporal features entirely
Use raw timestamp values directly
explain: Temporal features are cyclical (Monday is close to Sunday); sin/cos encoding preserves this structure better than linear day-of-week values.

q: An organization has imbalanced data: 99% negative class, 1% positive class for a fraud detection model. Simple random sampling for train/validation/test splits could result in minority class being absent from test set. What should be done?
options: Use stratified splitting to maintain class distribution in all sets
Use simple random splitting
Use only the majority class for all splits
Combine test and validation sets
explain: Stratified splitting ensures minority classes appear in all splits proportionally, enabling proper evaluation of minority class performance.

q: An ML engineer builds a feature store using SageMaker Feature Store to share features across multiple teams and models. What are the key benefits?
options: Slower feature development
Only benefits to feature storage
No real benefits compared to manual feature engineering
Centralized feature definitions, versioning, reusability, and both online/offline retrieval for training and inference
explain: Feature Store enables feature reusability, versioning, and centralized management, reducing redundant work and improving feature consistency.

q: An ML engineer trains a deep learning model on a dataset with 100,000 samples. The model has 50 million parameters. Overfitting is observed. What regularization technique is most effective?
options: Use a very high learning rate
Increase batch size to 10,000
Apply dropout, L2 regularization, and data augmentation to reduce model complexity and improve generalization
Remove all regularization
explain: Dropout and L2 regularization directly combat overfitting by reducing effective model capacity and penalizing large weights.

q: An ML engineer uses SageMaker Hyperparameter Tuning with Bayesian optimization to find optimal learning rate and batch size. After 100 trials, should tuning continue?
options: Check convergence: if best metric plateaued, stop tuning to save cost; continue if improvements are still being found
Tuning should never be used
Tuning is complete after 100 trials regardless
Always continue to 1000 trials
explain: Hyperparameter tuning should stop when optimization plateaus to balance model quality and computational cost.

q: An organization trains a recommendation model that predicts which product a user will buy next. The training data includes 10 million user-item interactions. The model shows high variance in validation metrics across k-fold splits. What could be done?
options: Reduce model complexity
Ignore variance; use mean metric only
Use only 1 fold instead of k-fold
Investigate data heterogeneity; consider increasing model capacity or using ensemble methods to reduce variance
explain: High variance across folds suggests data heterogeneity; ensemble methods or increased capacity can improve robustness across different data subsets.

q: An ML engineer trains a BERT model for sentiment analysis on Twitter data. The model is trained with a learning rate of 2e-5 (standard for BERT fine-tuning) for 3 epochs. Training loss decreases smoothly. How can overfitting on small Twitter dataset be prevented?
options: Train for 100 epochs on small data
Remove all regularization
Use early stopping based on validation loss and consider reducing training epochs or increasing regularization for small datasets
Use a much larger learning rate
explain: Early stopping on small datasets prevents overfitting by halting training before the model memorizes training data.

q: An ML engineer deploys a TensorFlow model to SageMaker using a custom container. To debug the deployment, the engineer needs to view the container logs. Where are these logs available?
options: SageMaker console only
Logs are not available for custom containers
CloudWatch Logs associated with the training/inference instance
Local notebook terminal only
explain: SageMaker containers stream logs to CloudWatch Logs, enabling remote debugging and monitoring without SSH access to instances.

q: An organization has a SageMaker endpoint that serves predictions. The endpoint needs to perform A/B testing with two model variants. Variant A should get 30% traffic, Variant B should get 70% traffic. How is this configured in SageMaker?
options: This is not possible in SageMaker
Manually implement routing logic in application code
Configure production variants with 0.3 and 0.7 traffic weights in endpoint configuration
Deploy two separate endpoints and use DNS round-robin
explain: SageMaker endpoint configuration supports production variants with traffic weights, enabling built-in A/B testing without external routing.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model endpoint in a VPC for security. The endpoint needs to access training data stored in S3. How should S3 access be configured?
options: Configure a NAT gateway for internet access
Use VPC S3 endpoint (gateway or interface endpoint) for private connectivity without internet gateway
S3 access from VPC endpoints is not possible
Use public S3 bucket accessible from internet
explain: VPC S3 endpoints provide private, secure access to S3 without routing traffic through internet gateway, maintaining network isolation.

q: An organization tracks model performance using SageMaker Model Monitor. An anomaly is detected: prediction distribution has shifted (mean changed from 0.5 to 0.6). What is the appropriate next step?
options: Disable monitoring
Investigate if this is concept drift (model behavior change) or data drift; retrain if concept drift is confirmed
Immediately replace the model
No action needed if accuracy is still high
explain: Prediction shift requires investigation; concept drift warrants retraining while data drift may require data collection strategy changes.

q: An ML model for hiring recommendations shows bias: 80% recommendation rate for male candidates, 40% for female candidates with identical qualifications. This violates fairness principles. How should this be addressed?
options: Use only female candidates
Ignore bias to avoid complexity
Manually review all predictions
Use SageMaker Clarify to quantify bias, retrain with fairness constraints or remove biased features
explain: SageMaker Clarify enables bias quantification and fairness-aware retraining to achieve equitable model decisions across demographics.

q: An ML engineer processes 50TB of image data for training. Images are stored in separate S3 buckets by region (us-east, eu-west, ap-southeast). To accelerate data loading during training, what approach is best?
options: Train from all regions in a single job
Download all data to instance storage
Train separately in each region
Consolidate data to a single S3 bucket in the same region as training instances to minimize data transfer latency
explain: Co-locating training instances with data (same region) minimizes data transfer latency and network costs for large-scale data training.

q: An organization's training data includes records with missing values: 5% missing in feature A, 20% missing in feature B, 50% missing in feature C. How should missing values be handled?
options: Replace all missing values with 0
Remove all rows with any missing values
Feature C with 50% missing should likely be removed; use imputation (mean/median) for features A and B with low missingness
Keep all missing values as-is
explain: High missingness (50%) in feature C reduces information value; low missingness (5-20%) can be imputed; severe missingness should be removed.

q: An ML engineer builds a multi-output regression model that predicts both temperature and humidity from meteorological data. How should the model be trained and evaluated?
options: Train a multi-output model; use joint loss function (sum of MSE for each output) and track metrics separately for each output
This is not possible
Train two separate single-output models
Combine outputs into single value
explain: Multi-output models can share learned representations across related tasks; joint training improves efficiency and may improve performance.

q: An ML engineer trains a neural network on 10,000 samples using batch size of 100, so 100 batches per epoch. How does the number of weight updates per epoch compare to SGD with batch size 1?
options: Mini-batch (100) has fewer updates per epoch (100 vs 10,000) but each update is based on more samples
Weight updates are unrelated to batch size
Same number of weight updates per epoch
Mini-batch has more weight updates
explain: Mini-batch training updates weights fewer times per epoch but each update averages gradients over multiple samples, improving stability.

q: An ML engineer manages 50+ SageMaker endpoints across multiple regions for a global application. Monitoring and updating each endpoint individually is time-consuming. How can this be simplified?
options: Consolidate all endpoints into one region
Use SageMaker Model Registry and CI/CD pipelines with CloudFormation to deploy and update endpoints across regions
Don't update endpoints
Manual management of each endpoint
explain: CI/CD with Model Registry and CloudFormation enables automated, consistent deployment and updates across multiple endpoints and regions.

q: A healthcare ML model predicts disease risk. Regulatory compliance requires maintaining an audit trail of decisions for 7 years. What should be monitored and logged?
options: Only accuracy metrics
Only final predictions
Input features, model version, prediction, confidence score, and timestamps for compliance auditing
No logging needed
explain: Complete audit trails with features, model versions, and predictions enable compliance verification and investigation of adverse outcomes.

q: An ML engineer applies min-max normalization to feature X (scaled to [0, 1]). New data arrives with value 150, but X was normalized based on training range [0, 100]. How should this be handled?
options: Investigate why new data exceeds training range (data drift); retrain if necessary or adjust normalization
Clip value to 1 without investigation
Normalize separately without retraining
Reject the data point
explain: Out-of-range values indicate data drift; investigation and potential model retraining ensures predictions remain reliable.

q: An organization processes text data for an NLP model. The raw text includes HTML tags, special characters, and mixed case. What is the correct preprocessing order?
options: Tokenize first without any cleaning
No preprocessing needed
Remove special characters, convert to lowercase, tokenize
Remove HTML tags first, then lowercase, tokenize, and optionally remove stop words
explain: HTML and special character removal before tokenization prevents noise; case conversion normalizes text; stop word removal reduces dimensionality.

q: An ML engineer trains a model with 30% dropout rate. During inference on a held-out test set, the engineer forgets to disable dropout. What will happen?
options: Predictions will be the same as training
Predictions will have high variance and differ across runs due to random dropout during inference
Dropout automatically disables
Performance will improve
explain: Dropout should be disabled during inference (eval mode); leaving it on causes random weight dropping, increasing prediction variance.

q: An ML engineer uses different learning rates for different layers in a BERT model: 1e-5 for BERT layers, 1e-3 for classification head. Why is this effective?
options: Different layers learn at different rates; fine-tuning BERT with low LR preserves pre-trained knowledge; higher LR for head enables quick adaptation
Lower learning rates are always better
This has no effect
Learning rates should be identical across all layers
explain: Layer-wise learning rates preserve pre-trained knowledge in early layers while allowing faster learning in untrained classification layers.

q: An ML engineer wants to serve a model trained on GPU (using CUDA) to CPU-only instances for cost savings. What needs to be done?
options: Retrain on CPU using CPU-compatible frameworks; use optimized inference libraries like TensorRT or ONNX for CPU inference
Deploy to GPU instance anyway
Models cannot run on CPU
CPU inference is not supported
explain: CPU-optimized inference libraries and frameworks enable serving GPU-trained models on CPU with acceptable latency and cost tradeoffs.

q: A deployed fraud detection model uses SageMaker Model Monitor. The data quality report shows: input feature X has increased missing rate from 2% to 15%, feature Y histogram shifted. What should be done?
options: Immediately retrain without investigation
Disable the monitoring
Investigate data quality degradation; check data pipeline for bugs; consider imputation strategy or model retraining
Ignore the report
explain: Data quality drift (increased missingness, distribution shift) requires investigation to identify root cause (data pipeline issues, upstream changes).

q: An ML engineer needs to create a balanced dataset for a binary classification task with 90:10 class imbalance. The minority class has only 1,000 samples. What is the recommended approach?
options: Ignore the imbalance
Use only the minority class
Oversample minority class to 9,000 samples and use undersampling/oversampling with stratified splits to maintain class balance
Use all 1,000 minority samples and 9,000 majority samples only
explain: Combined undersampling majority + oversampling minority (with stratified splits) creates balanced training data while preserving test set distribution.

q: An ML engineer trains an ensemble model (random forest with 100 trees). A single tree shows 95% accuracy on training data but 60% on test data. The ensemble shows 85% accuracy on training and 82% on test. Why does ensemble generalize better?
options: Ensemble has fewer trees
Single trees are always better
Ensembles always overfit
Ensemble averaging reduces individual tree overfitting; uncorrelated tree errors cancel out, improving generalization
explain: Ensemble methods reduce variance by averaging independent models; uncorrelated errors from multiple trees provide more robust predictions.

q: An ML engineer implements canary deployment for a new model version. 5% of traffic goes to the new model, 95% to the old model. After 1 hour, the new model shows 0.5% error rate vs 0.1% for the old model. What should happen?
options: Continue rolling out to 100% immediately
Rollback entirely
Halt rollout and investigate the error increase before proceeding
Ignore the difference; both are acceptable
explain: Canary deployments catch errors early; a 5x error increase warrants investigation before expanding to 100% traffic.

q: An ML model is deployed for a year with no changes. The team asks: 'Should we monitor for drift if the model is not being updated?' What is the best response?
options: Monitoring is optional
No monitoring needed if model is not changing
Only monitor when retrained
Yes, monitor for drift to detect when retraining is needed; lack of updates doesn't prevent data distribution from changing
explain: Data continues to evolve in production; drift monitoring identifies when static models become stale and retraining is necessary.

q: An ML engineer extracts 50 features from raw clickstream data using simple heuristics (feature counting, time aggregation). An Autopilot evaluation shows that using the original raw data with automatic feature engineering outperforms manual features. What does this suggest?
options: Automatic feature engineering discovered more predictive features; domain knowledge and algorithmic methods can be complementary
Manual feature engineering is always better
Features are not important
Using raw data is always worse
explain: Automatic feature engineering sometimes discovers non-obvious features manual engineers miss; both approaches have merits.

q: An ML engineer trains a gradient boosting model on 1 million samples. To reduce training time, the engineer trains on a 100,000-sample subset (10% of data). How might this affect model quality?
options: Sample size doesn't matter
Model quality may degrade; learning curves should be checked; if performance plateaus at 100k, subset is sufficient
Subset training always improves quality
No effect on model quality
explain: Learning curves reveal if 10% of data is sufficient; if performance plateaus early, subset training is efficient; if not, more data helps.

q: An ML engineer needs to deploy a model that requires GPU inference. SageMaker real-time endpoints support GPU instances, but async endpoints are cheaper. Which should be used?
options: GPU is not available in either option
Always use async for cost savings
Real-time if latency <1s is required; async if requests can wait and cost savings are prioritized
Real-time for all cases
explain: Real-time endpoints suit latency-critical applications; async endpoints reduce costs for tolerating longer delays.

q: A bank deploys a loan approval model. An audit reveals that the model uses salary (strongly predictive) but salary correlates with protected attributes (gender, race). Using salary indirectly discriminates. How should this be addressed?
options: Remove all correlated features
Continue using salary without changes
Use SageMaker Clarify to analyze if salary-driven predictions result in disparate impact; remove or constraint salary if discrimination is found
Ignore audits
explain: Indirect discrimination through correlated features can be detected and mitigated; fairness analysis and retraining with constraints ensures compliance.

q: An organization ingests customer transaction data with billions of records. Data quality checks must run before training. Which serverless option is most cost-effective for sporadic data validation jobs?
options: SageMaker Processing with on-demand instances
RDS database validation
Always-on EC2 instances
AWS Glue jobs that scale based on data volume processed
explain: AWS Glue scales cost to actual data processed without fixed instance costs, making it ideal for variable-frequency validation workloads.

q: An ML engineer prepares data for a time-series forecasting model. The data includes daily sales from 500 stores over 5 years. How should temporal structure be preserved when splitting train/validation/test?
options: Combine all stores into single series
Random shuffle all data then split 80/10/10
Use chronological split: earlier data for training, later data for validation/test to simulate realistic forecasting
Split by store ID, not time
explain: Time-series must be split chronologically to avoid look-ahead bias and accurately simulate real deployment where models predict future data.

q: An ML engineer trains a classification model with extremely imbalanced data (99:1 class ratio). Standard accuracy metric shows 99% even with a naive model that predicts only the majority class. What metric should be tracked instead?
options: Accuracy only
Inference time
F1-score, precision, recall, and ROC-AUC to focus on minority class performance
Training loss
explain: F1-score and ROC-AUC are robust to class imbalance; accuracy is misleading when minority class is important.

q: An ML engineer trains a computer vision model using transfer learning from ImageNet pre-training. The engineer wants to fine-tune the model on a small dataset (1,000 images). What is the best approach?
options: Freeze early layers, fine-tune later layers with low learning rate to prevent catastrophic forgetting
Remove pre-trained weights and train from scratch
Train all layers with high learning rate
Use only ImageNet predictions without further training
explain: Fine-tuning with frozen early layers preserves pre-trained feature detectors while adapting to new task, improving small-data performance.

q: An ML engineer deploys models to both SageMaker endpoints and mobile devices (using SageMaker Edge). Model versions must be kept synchronized. How can this be managed?
options: Manual file transfer
Register models in SageMaker Model Registry; use version tags to sync both endpoints and Edge devices
Deploy separately to each platform
No synchronization needed
explain: Model Registry provides single source of truth; versions can be deployed to both endpoints and Edge devices via CI/CD pipelines.

q: A recommendation model is deployed. After 3 months, A/B test results show: new model CTR=3.5%, old model CTR=3.2%. Is the new model statistically significantly better?
options: No, old model is better
Yes, 0.3% difference is significant
Need to check statistical significance with confidence intervals and sample size; 0.3% difference may be noise
Statistics don't matter for recommendations
explain: Small absolute differences require statistical testing; with large traffic, small differences can be significant; with small traffic, they may be noise.

q: An organization collects real-time streaming sensor data from manufacturing equipment. Data arrives with occasional gaps (equipment downtime) and missing values. How should this be handled in the data lake?
options: Store missing values as null without handling
Reject all records with missing values
Impute missing values using forward-fill (carry forward last value) or interpolation; mark downtime periods explicitly
Use only complete records
explain: Forward-fill and interpolation are appropriate for sensor data; explicit downtime markers preserve data integrity and prevent training bias.

q: An ML team uses AWS Glue Catalog to store metadata for training data. Different teams add new datasets daily. How should data governance be enforced?
options: Manual governance by one person
Governance is not needed for Glue
Define naming conventions, tagging policies, and access controls in Glue Catalog; use Lake Formation for fine-grained permissions
No governance; anyone can add anything
explain: Lake Formation enables centralized governance: tagging policies, lineage tracking, and role-based access control for Glue data.

q: An ML engineer notices that a model's performance on the training set is 90% but on validation set drops to 75%. This gap indicates a specific issue. What is the most likely cause?
options: Overfitting; model learned training data specifics rather than generalizable patterns
Underfitting due to model simplicity
The loss function is incorrect
The model is too large
explain: Large training-validation gap is the primary indicator of overfitting; the model memorizes rather than generalizes.

q: An ML engineer trains a language model using distributed data parallelism across 8 GPU instances. After synchronizing gradients, one instance is significantly slower. How should this be addressed?
options: Ignore the slower instance
Identify and fix straggler (network issue, resource contention); replace instance if hardware-faulty
Use only 7 instances
Stop training
explain: Stragglers in distributed training bottleneck synchronization; investigation and replacement/repair prevents training slowdowns.

q: An ML engineer deploys a SageMaker endpoint but notices that for some requests, the response time is 10ms while for others it's 5 seconds. This inconsistency is problematic for real-time applications. What could cause this?
options: Normal variance in network latency
Endpoint is hitting resource limits and queuing requests; some hit cold starts; need to increase capacity or auto-scaling
The model is non-deterministic
This is expected behavior
explain: High variance in latency typically indicates resource saturation; increasing capacity or enabling aggressive auto-scaling can stabilize response times.

q: An ML-based system processes patient medical records for diagnosis support. A clinician notices the model occasionally makes counterintuitive recommendations. The team should ensure explainability. What should be logged?
options: No additional logging needed
Only predictions and outcomes
Model architecture only
Input features, key factors influencing prediction, confidence scores, and reasoning for clinical validation
explain: Clinical explainability requires logging input features and factors influencing predictions for clinician review and model validation.

q: An organization has raw customer feedback text in multiple languages (English, Spanish, French). The text will train a multilingual sentiment classifier. What preprocessing step is critical?
options: Remove all non-English text
Combine all languages without marking
Use language-agnostic preprocessing only
Detect language for each text; apply language-specific tokenization and normalization; consider language as a feature
explain: Language detection enables appropriate tokenization and preprocessing; language as a feature helps the model learn language-specific patterns.

q: An ML engineer trains a neural network model using SGD with momentum=0.9. Training shows good convergence initially but oscillates near the minimum. How can this be improved?
options: Remove momentum
Use larger learning rate
Switch to Adam optimizer which adapts learning rate per parameter, potentially reducing oscillations
Increase momentum to 0.95
explain: Adam optimizer combines momentum with adaptive learning rates, often providing more stable convergence than SGD with fixed momentum.

q: An ML engineer exports a trained model to ONNX format for deployment on edge devices. The original model was trained in PyTorch with GPU operations (CUDA). What validation is needed?
options: No validation; ONNX export is transparent
Skip ONNX and use PyTorch directly on devices
Test ONNX model output matches PyTorch on CPU; validate edge device inference accuracy and latency
Validation is not necessary
explain: ONNX conversion can lose numerical precision or operations; testing ensures ONNX model behaves identically to original on target devices.

q: An ML model deployed 2 years ago has never been retrained. The business reports that model performance has slowly degraded. What should the team do?
options: Deploy a completely different model
Immediately retrain without understanding why
Continue using the old model
Implement data/concept drift monitoring; retrain if drift detected; establish retraining schedule
explain: Drift monitoring identifies degradation; understanding whether it's data or concept drift informs retraining strategy and prevents waste.

q: An ML engineer applies Principal Component Analysis (PCA) to reduce 1,000 features to 100 principal components. This significantly reduces training time. Is there a risk?
options: PCA always improves model accuracy
PCA components are harder to interpret; original feature importance is lost; ensure variance explained is sufficient (>95%)
No risk; more features are always removed
Dimensionality reduction has no tradeoffs
explain: PCA trades interpretability for computational efficiency; high variance explained (>95%) ensures minimal information loss.

q: An ML engineer trains a classification model and observes that the learning curve shows training and validation accuracy are both low even with more data. What does this indicate?
options: The model has high bias (underfitting); model is too simple for the task
The model is overfitting
The data is perfect
More data alone will solve it
explain: When both training and validation accuracy are low, the model lacks capacity to fit even training data (high bias/underfitting).

q: An ML team deploys models using SageMaker Pipelines with automated retraining triggered monthly. A retraining run fails. What should happen?
options: Disable retraining
Configure pipeline to alert on failure; manually investigate and fix before retraining continues
Continue using last successful model without alerting
Deploy the broken model anyway
explain: Pipeline failures indicate issues (data quality, drift, training bugs); alerts enable manual investigation and prevent deploying broken models.

q: An organization's training data has 5 million records. An ML engineer needs to create a data sample for rapid model prototyping. Should they use random sampling or stratified sampling?
options: Use stratified sampling if target variable is imbalanced; stratified ensures minority class remains represented in sample
Sampling is unnecessary
Random sampling is always fine
Use only majority class for speed
explain: Stratified sampling preserves target distribution; critical for imbalanced data to avoid biased prototyping on majority class only.

q: An ML model for customer churn prediction is in production. The business asks: 'How confident are we in the model's churn predictions?' How should this be quantified?
options: Tell them accuracy percentage
Tell them the model is trained
Track calibration: compare predicted probability vs actual churn rate; well-calibrated models have reliable confidence scores
Confidence cannot be measured
explain: Model calibration measures if predicted probabilities match true probabilities; uncalibrated models give false confidence.

q: An ML engineer processes images for training but discovers that some images are corrupted (truncated PNG files). How should these be handled?
options: Detect and remove corrupted images before training to prevent errors and ensure data quality
Store corrupted images separately
Convert all to a standard format that accepts corruption
Include them and let the model handle errors
explain: Corrupted images prevent training; detection and removal ensures clean training data and reproducible models.

q: An ML engineer trains a seq2seq model for machine translation. The model shows good BLEU score on in-domain test data but poor performance on out-of-domain text. What technique can help?
options: This is expected and acceptable
Increase model size arbitrarily
Only deploy on in-domain data
Use domain adaptation: train on mix of in-domain and out-of-domain data; or fine-tune on target domain
explain: Domain adaptation exposes the model to both domains during training, improving generalization to target domain.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model for real-time fraud detection. Latency requirement is <100ms. The model runs inference in 50ms on ml.p3 GPU instances but costs $3/hour. CPU instances run inference in 150ms for $0.10/hour. What should be used?
options: Latency doesn't matter
Use GPU instances despite cost
Use neither
Use CPU if business can tolerate 150ms; otherwise optimize model (quantization, distillation) to meet latency on cheaper instance
explain: GPU meets latency SLA but costs 30x more; model optimization enables meeting latency on cheaper CPU instances.

q: A production ML system tracks 50 metrics in CloudWatch. The team is overwhelmed with alerts. How should alerting be prioritized?
options: Alert on every metric
Define critical metrics (accuracy, latency, error rate) with meaningful thresholds; alert only on actionable anomalies
Disable all alerts to reduce noise
Random alerting
explain: Alert fatigue reduces effectiveness; focus on critical metrics with thresholds that indicate real problems requiring investigation.

q: An ML engineer trains a recommendation model on user-item interactions. New users with no interaction history appear in production. How should this cold-start problem be addressed?
options: Exclude new users entirely
Use content-based features, popularity-based recommendations, or hybrid approaches for cold-start
The model cannot handle new users
Train a separate model for new users
explain: Cold-start mitigation includes content-based fallbacks and popularity-based defaults until user gets interaction history.

q: An ML engineer compares two models: Model A uses all 100 features, Model B uses 10 hand-selected features. Model A achieves 85% accuracy, Model B achieves 83%. Which is preferable for production?
options: Both are equally good
Accuracy is the only metric that matters
Always use Model A (higher accuracy)
Model B may be preferable: lower feature complexity, faster inference, easier to explain; 2% accuracy loss may be acceptable
explain: Simpler models (fewer features) reduce maintenance, improve interpretability, and may be preferred despite slight accuracy decrease.

q: An ML team uses GitOps to manage SageMaker Pipeline definitions stored in Git. A developer merges a pipeline change that breaks the pipeline. How should this be prevented?
options: No validation; deploy directly from Git
Implement CI/CD pipeline to validate and test definitions before merging; use pull request reviews
No version control for pipelines
Manual deployment only
explain: CI/CD validation and PR reviews catch pipeline definition errors before deployment, preventing production outages.

q: A machine learning model's predictions are used for high-stakes decisions (loan approval). The team must explain individual decisions to customers and regulators. What is the best approach?
options: Provide only final prediction
Use SageMaker Clarify to generate SHAP explanations showing feature importance for each prediction
Model decisions are unexplainable
Provide raw model weights
explain: SHAP explanations show feature contributions to individual predictions, providing transparent, interpretable explanations for stakeholders.

q: An ML engineer needs to extract numerical features from unstructured text (e.g., product reviews) for a regression model. What approach should be used?
options: Extract features: text length, sentiment scores, keyword presence, TF-IDF vectors, or embeddings from pre-trained models
Text cannot be used for regression
Use raw text directly in numeric model
Ignore text and use only structured features
explain: Text features (sentiment, TF-IDF, embeddings) are derived numerically from raw text, enabling their use in standard ML models.

q: An ML engineer applies log transformation to a right-skewed feature (e.g., income distribution). This makes the feature approximately normal. Why is this useful?
options: Log transformation is always necessary
Many algorithms (linear regression, logistic regression) perform better with normalized distributions; log transformation normalizes skewed data
Normal distribution has no benefits
Normalization has no effect on models
explain: Algorithms assuming normality (linear models) perform better; log transformation reduces skewness and stabilizes variance.

q: An ML engineer trains a model with batch normalization. During training, batch norm uses moving averages of mean/variance. During inference, batch norm uses population statistics. Why this difference?
options: Population statistics are used during training
Batch norm has no effect on inference
Training uses batch statistics (varying per batch); inference uses accumulated population statistics for deterministic predictions
No difference; same statistics are used
explain: Batch norm must use population statistics during inference to avoid variance from small batch effects, ensuring stable predictions.

q: An ML engineer needs to deploy a 5GB model to SageMaker endpoints. The endpoint should start within 30 seconds of receiving the first request. Is this feasible?
options: Endpoints always meet latency SLA
No; loading 5GB takes >30s; use provisioned endpoints with pre-loaded models or reduce model size via quantization/distillation
Load time is irrelevant
Yes, always feasible
explain: Model loading time depends on size and instance speed; large models may exceed latency SLA; quantization or pre-warming required.

q: A model deployed for customer recommendation shows bias: recommendations favor products from majority-owned companies. How should this be diagnosed and fixed?
options: Remove all recommendations
Bias is unavoidable in ML
Use SageMaker Clarify to analyze bias in recommendations; retrain with fairness constraints or adjust business rules
Ignore bias if accuracy is high
explain: Fairness analysis reveals bias; retraining with fairness objectives or rule adjustments ensures equitable recommendations.
