q: An ML engineer prepares data for a time-series model by creating windows of 30 consecutive days as features predicting the next day's target. A sliding window approach is used. What is a potential issue?
options: No issues; windows are independent
Sliding windows improve accuracy
Windows don't affect data independence
Overlapping windows in train/validation/test cause data leakage; validation/test windows may contain training data
explain: Sliding windows with temporal data create overlap; proper splitting (no overlap between train/validation/test) prevents leakage.

q: An ML engineer fine-tunes a BERT model on a domain-specific dataset (medical texts). The task is medical entity recognition. Which loss function should be used?
options: Binary cross-entropy (BCL)
Categorical cross-entropy for multi-class token classification (disease, drug, symptom, etc.)
No loss function needed
Mean squared error (MSE)
explain: Multi-class token classification uses categorical cross-entropy; BERT outputs probabilities over entity classes for each token.

q: An organization uses multi-model SageMaker endpoints where multiple models share the same instance for cost savings. When should multi-model endpoints be preferred over single-model?
options: Always use multi-model
Never use multi-model
Multi-model when models have similar response characteristics and traffic patterns; shared instance utilization is efficient
Model consolidation is not possible
explain: Multi-model endpoints are efficient when different models have complementary traffic patterns (e.g., peak at different times).

q: A model's prediction latency has gradually increased from 50ms to 200ms over 6 months. The team never updated the model. What could cause this?
options: This is normal
The model complexity increased
The instance type changed
Data drift may require model retraining; stale models sometimes accumulate more compute as input distributions change
explain: While model code didn't change, concept drift may require retraining; also, gradual endpoint resource contention or instance degradation causes latency creep.

q: An ML engineer prepares data where timestamps are in milliseconds since epoch. Should these raw values be used as model features?
options: No; derive meaningful features like hour, day, month, or encode cyclicity; raw timestamps don't capture temporal patterns
Timestamps should never be used
Yes, raw timestamps are numerical and should work
Timestamps are always interpretable
explain: Raw timestamps (large, arbitrary values) provide no interpretability; derived temporal features (hour, day) capture periodic patterns.

q: An ML engineer evaluates two models using cross-validation. Model A: mean CV accuracy=85%, std=15%. Model B: mean CV accuracy=83%, std=2%. Which model is more reliable?
options: Model A due to higher mean
Variance doesn't matter
Model B due to lower variance across folds; consistent performance is more reliable than high variance
Both are equally reliable
explain: Lower CV variance indicates consistent generalization across data subsets; high variance suggests performance depends on specific fold splits.

q: An ML engineer needs to update a SageMaker endpoint's model without causing downtime. The new model is ready in Model Registry. How can this be done safely?
options: Deploy to separate endpoint and switch DNS
Update endpoint immediately, causing brief downtime
Manual transition of requests
Update endpoint with shadow variant, route subset of traffic, verify, then swap traffic weights
explain: Shadow variants enable safe testing; traffic is routed to new model while old model still handles main traffic, minimizing risk.

q: An organization trains on data with strong seasonality (e.g., retail sales spike during holidays). A simple train/test split by random sampling may result in test set with different seasonal composition. What split strategy is better?
options: Combine all seasons in training
Ignore seasonality in splits
Random split is fine
Chronological split ensures test set has representative seasonal periods
explain: Chronological splits ensure test set includes all seasonal periods, avoiding bias toward specific seasons.

q: An ML engineer works with an organizational data catalog (AWS Glue Catalog). A dataset lacks documentation. How should this be addressed?
options: Create separate documentation in Word
Document schema, data quality, update frequency, and business context in Glue Catalog; add tags for discoverability
Documentation is unnecessary
Use undocumented data anyway
explain: Glue Catalog metadata (schema, tags, descriptions) ensures data governance and enables other teams to reuse data appropriately.

q: An ML engineer applies label smoothing to a classification model where class labels are one-hot encoded. Instead of [0,1,0], labels become [0.05, 0.9, 0.05]. How does this help?
options: Label smoothing prevents overconfidence, improves generalization, and enables better uncertainty estimates
It makes the model less accurate
No effect on training
Smoothing is only for regression
explain: Label smoothing encourages the model to be less confident, preventing overfitting and improving calibration for uncertainty estimates.

q: An ML engineer wants to replicate a SageMaker endpoint across multiple AWS regions for disaster recovery and low-latency global serving. What is the best approach?
options: Manual replication in each region
Replication is not possible
Deploy to one region only
Register model in Model Registry; use CloudFormation or Terraform to deploy endpoints across regions consistently
explain: Model Registry + IaC (CloudFormation/Terraform) enables consistent endpoint replication across regions for global availability.

q: A deployed model shows good validation performance but poor production performance. The team investigates and finds that production data has different feature distributions than training data. What is this called?
options: Overfitting
Covariate shift or feature drift; model performs poorly when input distribution changes
Underfitting
Model corruption
explain: Covariate shift (input distribution change) causes performance drop even if the input-output relationship unchanged.

q: An ML engineer applies standard scaling (z-score normalization) to features before training a tree-based model (random forest). Is this necessary?
options: Yes, always normalize before any model
Scaling helps tree models slightly
Scaling has unknown effect on trees
No; tree-based models are invariant to feature scaling (they use splits, not distances)
explain: Tree models use feature splits, not distances; scaling irrelevant for them but necessary for distance-based (KNN, SVM, neural networks) models.

q: An ML engineer uses random search for hyperparameter tuning instead of grid search. With 5 hyperparameters and 3 values each, grid search tests 243 combinations while random search tests 50. What is the advantage of random search here?
options: Grid search is always better
Random search is only faster, not better
Random search with 50 samples covers parameter space more efficiently than small grid; often finds better solutions with fewer trials
Both are identical
explain: Random search efficiently explores high-dimensional spaces; with limited budget, it often outperforms grid search covering fewer grid points.

q: An ML engineer receives a request to deploy a model predicting medical diagnosis. The predictions will influence treatment decisions. What governance step is critical before deployment?
options: Skip validation to save time
Only check training accuracy
Deploy immediately without checks
Validate model accuracy on external test set; conduct bias analysis; document limitations and get clinical review approval
explain: High-stakes medical models require external validation, bias analysis, and clinical review to ensure safety and fairness before deployment.

q: An ML engineer extracts features from customer account lifetime (10 years of transaction data). The feature set becomes very large. What technique can reduce dimensionality while preserving information?
options: Dimensionality cannot be reduced
Use PCA, feature selection, or domain knowledge to select top predictive features; eliminate redundant/correlated features
Use all features regardless of size
Remove all features
explain: Feature selection (statistical tests, mutual information) and PCA reduce dimensionality while preserving predictive information.

q: An ML engineer trains a model and observes that training loss decreases smoothly but validation loss increases after epoch 10. What is the appropriate action?
options: Continue training to epoch 100
Increase regularization retroactively
Decrease learning rate
Use early stopping at epoch 10 to prevent overfitting; the increasing validation loss indicates no further improvement
explain: Early stopping halts training when validation performance stops improving (or degrades), preventing overfitting and saving compute.

q: An ML team manages 30 SageMaker endpoints with different models. The business requires that any model update be reviewed and approved before deployment. How should this process be automated?
options: Manual approval for each endpoint
Use SageMaker Model Registry with approval workflows; CI/CD pipeline runs tests and gates deployment on approval
Skip approval to speed deployment
Approval is not possible
explain: Model Registry approval workflows enforce governance; CI/CD pipeline gates prevent deploying unapproved models to production.

q: A credit scoring model is monitored and shows no change in accuracy metrics for 6 months. However, the approval rate for loan applications has increased 10%. What could explain this without accuracy changing?
options: Accuracy guarantees approval rate stability
Prediction distribution may have shifted (more high-score predictions); threshold adjustment or business rule changes shifted approval rate
This is impossible
The model is unchanged
explain: Accuracy measures overall correctness; approval rate depends on prediction distribution and decision threshold, which can shift without accuracy change.

q: An ML engineer prepares historical customer data for training. Some records contain PII (personally identifiable information) that should be removed before sharing with ML teams. What is the best approach?
options: Store PII separately without linking to data
Use SageMaker Data Wrangler or AWS Glue DataBrew to mask/redact PII while preserving useful information
Leave PII in data; ML teams need all information
Remove entire records with PII
explain: PII masking (SSN hashing, name anonymization) removes sensitive information while preserving data utility for training.

q: An ML engineer trains a transformer model on 10 million sentences. The model has 300M parameters. Gradient accumulation is used with effective batch size of 10,000. Why use gradient accumulation?
options: Reduces model accuracy
Simulates large batch training on memory-limited hardware; improves stability and allows larger effective batch size
Accumulation has no benefit
Slower convergence
explain: Gradient accumulation enables training with large effective batch sizes on memory-constrained GPUs, improving convergence stability.

q: An ML engineer deploys models in a production environment with strict security requirements: no internet access, encrypted data at rest/in-transit. How should SageMaker endpoints be configured?
options: Deploy in private VPC subnet; use S3 VPC endpoint for model artifact access; encrypt artifacts and network traffic
Deploy to public subnet with internet access
Security requirements are incompatible with SageMaker
Skip encryption for performance
explain: SageMaker supports VPC deployment with private subnets, S3 VPC endpoints, and encryption for meeting strict security requirements.

q: A model is deployed and monitored. SageMaker Model Monitor shows that the model is predicting values outside the training data range (e.g., predicting salary >$10M when training max was $500K). What should be done?
options: Disable the model
Investigate if this indicates concept drift or if the model learned an undesirable extrapolation; consider retraining or adding constraints
This is normal
Accept the predictions
explain: Out-of-range predictions often indicate the model has learned problematic extrapolation patterns; investigation and retraining may be needed.

q: An ML engineer handles a dataset with extreme outliers: most features are in [0, 100] but occasional values reach 10,000. Should outliers be removed or transformed?
options: Outliers have no impact on models
Investigate outliers (are they errors or real?); if real, apply robust scaling (RobustScaler) that's less sensitive to extremes
Keep all outliers without addressing
Always remove outliers
explain: RobustScaler using median/IQR handles outliers better than standard scaling; investigation determines if outliers are valid or errors.

q: An ML team builds a churn prediction model. Training data includes customer behavior from 2021. Will this model work well on 2024 data without retraining?
options: Always retrain monthly regardless
Retraining is never needed
Unlikely; customer preferences and business context evolve; model will experience concept drift and should be retrained periodically
Yes, customer behavior is stable over time
explain: Concept drift (changing patterns over 3 years) requires periodic retraining; drift monitoring identifies when retraining is necessary.

q: An ML engineer trains a recommendation model that uses user embeddings and item embeddings learned jointly. After training, the engineer can use these embeddings for downstream tasks. This approach is called:
options: Feature engineering only
It's not possible to reuse embeddings
Representation learning or transfer learning; embeddings capture learned features for reuse in other tasks
Embeddings are only for the original task
explain: Learned embeddings encode useful representations that transfer to other tasks, enabling efficient model development and better generalization.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model to production and discovers it requires custom inference code (pre/post-processing). Should this be packaged in a custom container or as SageMaker SDK code?
options: Custom container only
Pre/post-processing is not possible
Package custom code in Docker container and deploy via SageMaker; ensures reproducibility and handles pre/post-processing
Custom code on user's application side only
explain: Custom containers encapsulate inference logic; deployment via SageMaker ensures consistency and simplifies inference architecture.

q: A healthcare prediction model's decisions are logged for compliance. An audit requires: 'Explain why this patient was classified as high-risk.' The team should use:
options: Patient's raw features only
Tell auditors the model is accurate
SageMaker Clarify SHAP explanations showing which features drove the high-risk prediction for that specific patient
The model cannot be explained
explain: SHAP explanations provide patient-specific feature importance, enabling auditable, explainable decisions required for compliance.

q: An ML team works with large unstructured text documents (contracts, reports). Before ML training, what is the essential first step?
options: Text is unsuitable for ML
Preprocess: remove metadata/formatting, tokenize, clean noise; extract structured features or embeddings for training
Remove all documents >1 page
Use text directly in models
explain: Text preprocessing (cleaning, tokenization, feature extraction) is essential before ML training to convert unstructured text to usable form.

q: An ML engineer trains a neural network but notices that gradients during backpropagation become very small (vanishing gradients). This prevents early layers from learning. How can this be addressed?
options: Vanishing gradients are unavoidable
Use batch normalization or residual connections to stabilize gradient flow through deep networks
Increase learning rate to compensate
Use a shallower network only
explain: Batch normalization and residual connections maintain stable gradient magnitudes through deep networks, enabling effective deep learning.

q: An ML engineer manages endpoints using SageMaker Pipelines for continuous deployment. The pipeline is triggered automatically when new training data arrives. What risk exists?
options: Continuous deployment is not possible
Continuous deployment ensures always-fresh models
No risk with automation
Risk of deploying broken/biased models; pipeline should include automated tests (accuracy, bias checks) before deployment
explain: Automated deployment without validation risks pushing broken models; quality gates (accuracy thresholds, bias checks) are essential.

q: An ML model's predictions are used to decide which job candidates to interview. The model is monitored and is found to be biased: 60% of majority group candidates are recommended vs 40% for minority group. What is the issue?
options: Disparate impact (different outcomes for protected groups); use SageMaker Clarify to quantify bias and retrain with fairness objectives
This is acceptable if accuracy is high
Bias is unavoidable
Only accuracy matters
explain: Disparate impact (different approval rates across groups) is illegal discrimination; fairness analysis and retraining are required.

q: An ML engineer ingests time-series data with seasonal patterns (e.g., daily temperature). To train a forecasting model, what is critical?
options: Seasonality is irrelevant for time-series
Remove seasonal patterns as noise
Preserve seasonality by not removing trends; explicitly model seasonal components or use seasonal decomposition
Combine all seasons into single trend
explain: Seasonal patterns are predictive; modeling them (seasonal ARIMA, seasonal neural networks) improves forecast accuracy.

q: An ML engineer trains a model on heavily imbalanced data (1% positive class). The model achieves 99% accuracy. What metric should the engineer focus on instead?
options: Inference speed
Accuracy is the right metric
Training loss
F1-score, precision-recall curve, or ROC-AUC focus on minority class performance and avoid misleading high accuracy
explain: Accuracy is misleading on imbalanced data (predicting all negatives achieves high accuracy); use F1-score or ROC-AUC instead.

q: An ML team uses GitOps for model deployment. A developer accidentally commits a model with poor performance. How should this be prevented?
options: Skip validation to speed deployment
No checks; deploy immediately
Implement pre-commit hooks and CI/CD validation to test model performance before merging
Manual review only
explain: CI/CD validation gates prevent bad models from reaching main branch; pre-commit hooks catch issues early in development.

q: A deployed model is monitored using SageMaker Model Monitor. The baseline report shows: model accuracy 85%. New evaluation shows: accuracy 83%. Should the model be retrained?
options: Yes, immediately retrain
Accuracy changes are not monitored
No, investigate: 2% drop may be normal variance; check if statistical significance, drift type, or sample size explains it
Ignore the drop
explain: Small accuracy drops require investigation; not all drops warrant retraining; understanding cause informs decision.

q: An ML team discovers that the same customer appears multiple times in training data with different IDs (data duplication). How should this be handled?
options: Deduplicate based on identifying information; ensure train/test don't share customers (prevent leakage)
Duplicates improve model generalization
Ignore duplication
Keep duplicates to increase dataset size
explain: Duplicates inflate dataset size without adding information; deduplication prevents train/test leakage where same customers appear in both.

q: An ML engineer uses a pre-trained language model for a new task. The model is fine-tuned on small task-specific data (100 examples). What regularization is important?
options: Regularization harms transfer learning
No regularization needed for fine-tuning
Remove all constraints to maximize accuracy
Strong regularization (dropout, L2, early stopping) to prevent overfitting on small fine-tuning dataset
explain: Fine-tuning on small data easily overfits; strong regularization and early stopping preserve pre-trained knowledge while adapting to new task.

q: An ML engineer wants to serve a model on edge devices with limited compute (ARM CPU, 256MB RAM). The original model is 500MB. What approach enables this?
options: Edge deployment is not possible
Deploy full model anyway
Use model compression: quantization (int8), pruning, distillation into smaller student models
Larger models always perform better
explain: Model compression (quantization, pruning, distillation) reduces size and compute, enabling deployment on resource-constrained devices.

q: A fraud detection model is deployed. The business team reports: 'False positive rate increased from 1% to 5%.' This causes too many legitimate transactions to be flagged. What should be done?
options: Investigate: lowering decision threshold increased false positives; retrain with cost-sensitive learning or adjust threshold
Ignore the increase
Deploy a new random model
Increase threshold arbitrarily
explain: False positive rate increase suggests threshold is too low or model has drifted; retraining or threshold adjustment addresses the issue.

q: An ML engineer applies SMOTE (Synthetic Minority Over-sampling Technique) to generate synthetic samples for the minority class. After applying SMOTE to training data, should it be applied to validation/test data?
options: No; SMOTE only on training data to create realistic synthetic examples; validation/test remain unchanged and representative
SMOTE should be skipped entirely
Yes, apply SMOTE to all data
Test data should be oversampled more than training
explain: SMOTE generates synthetic training samples; test data must remain realistic and unmodified to accurately evaluate model performance.

q: An ML engineer trains a model with batch size 128. On the same hardware with batch size 256, training converges faster but final accuracy is lower. What is happening?
options: Larger batch sizes reduce variance in gradient estimates (faster convergence) but may get stuck in sharper minima (lower accuracy)
Lower accuracy is impossible with larger batch
Larger batch size always improves both speed and accuracy
Batch size doesn't affect convergence
explain: Larger batches reduce stochastic noise (faster convergence) but can hurt generalization; batch size affects both speed and accuracy.

q: An ML team manages models in SageMaker Model Registry. A model should not be deployed until it passes:  accuracy >90%, no bias (fairness metric >0.8), latency <100ms. How should this be enforced?
options: Manual checks by ML team
Skip checks to speed deployment
Checks are not enforceable
Automation: define SageMaker Pipelines conditional steps that test these criteria before deploying; only deploy if all pass
explain: Pipeline conditional steps enforce quality gates; models deploy only if all criteria pass, preventing bad models from reaching production.

q: An ML engineer works with customer behavioral data across multiple time zones. Timestamps are in UTC. What is important for feature extraction?
options: Time zones have no effect on features
Consider customer's local timezone when extracting time-based features (hour of day, day of week) to match local context
Ignore time zones; use UTC directly
Convert all to single timezone arbitrarily
explain: Time-based features should reflect customer's local time (peak shopping hours vary by timezone); UTC features lack local context.

q: An ML engineer trains a model on data from 2020-2022. In 2023, the model's performance drops significantly. The team checks: are features different? Yes. Are targets different? No. What is this?
options: Concept drift
Model bug
Overfitting
Covariate shift (data drift): input distribution changed but input-output relationship remains the same
explain: Covariate shift occurs when P(X) changes but P(Y|X) stays same; only input distribution drifted, not the causal relationship.

q: An ML engineer deploys models to SageMaker endpoints for 50 different customer segments, each with its own model. Managing 50 endpoints individually is tedious. How can this scale?
options: Eliminate models
Register all models in Model Registry with segment tags; use SageMaker Pipelines to deploy/update all segment models in parallel
Consolidate to single model
Manually manage each endpoint
explain: Model Registry with tags and Pipelines enable batch management of multiple models, reducing manual overhead for many endpoints.

q: A model predicts which students will drop out of college. The model is audited and found to systematically underpredict dropout for low-income students. What is the issue?
options: This is a systematic bias (disparate impact for low-income group); the model discriminates based on a proxy for protected status
Bias doesn't exist if accuracy is high
Fairness checks are unnecessary
Model accuracy is still high, so it's acceptable
explain: Disparate impact based on income (proxy for other protected attributes) is discriminatory; fairness analysis and retraining required.

q: An ML engineer works with tabular data containing both numeric and categorical features. The categorical feature 'country' has 195 unique values. Should one-hot encoding be used?
options: Yes, one-hot encode all categories
Remove categorical features entirely
No; one-hot encoding 195 categories creates 195 sparse columns; use target encoding, label encoding, or embedding approaches
Combine countries into regions
explain: High-cardinality one-hot encoding creates curse of dimensionality; target encoding or embeddings are more efficient.

q: An ML engineer evaluates a model using 5-fold cross-validation. Fold 1 accuracy: 90%, Fold 2: 88%, Fold 3: 92%, Fold 4: 85%, Fold 5: 89%. What is the CV accuracy and what does the variance tell us?
options: All folds have identical accuracy
CV accuracy = 88%, high variance indicates unstable model performance across data subsets
CV accuracy = 88.8%, variance of 2.6 indicates stable performance across folds
Variance is irrelevant
explain: Mean CV accuracy = (90+88+92+85+89)/5 = 88.8%; low variance indicates robust, stable generalization.

q: An ML team uses SageMaker Pipelines for automated model deployment. The pipeline runs daily, retrains, evaluates, and conditionally deploys. What should be monitored to ensure reliability?
options: Nothing; pipelines are self-sufficient
Monitor pipeline execution logs, failure rates, model quality metrics (accuracy drift), and deployment success; alert on failures
Skip monitoring to reduce overhead
Pipelines cannot fail
explain: Automated pipelines require monitoring pipeline health and model quality; failures need investigation to prevent bad deployments.

q: A model is deployed and serves 1M requests/day. SageMaker Model Monitor detects data drift. The team wants to understand: 'Which features are drifting?' What should be checked?
options: Drift cannot be attributed to specific features
Overall accuracy only
Model Monitor reports feature-level statistics (mean, distribution) comparing baseline to recent data; identify which features drifted
All features drift equally
explain: Model Monitor provides feature-level drift metrics; identifying which features drifted guides investigation and potential retraining.

q: An ML engineer builds a dataset for sentiment analysis containing product reviews of varying lengths (10-5000 words). How should text length variation be handled during training?
options: Use only the first 10 words of each review
Remove all reviews longer than 512 tokens
Keep variable lengths; use padding/truncation to uniform length for batch processing
Text length is irrelevant
explain: Padding/truncation standardizes input length for batch processing; preserves information while enabling efficient batching.

q: An organization's customer database contains millions of records with occasional missing values in key features. An ML engineer must decide: drop rows with missing values or impute them?
options: Always drop rows with any missing values
Replace all missing values with 0
Impute using mean/median/mode for numeric features; use categorical mode or create 'missing' category for categorical features
Missing values should remain
explain: Imputation preserves sample size and information; dropping rows wastes data unless missingness is systematic.

q: An ML engineer trains a model with 1000 epochs but uses early stopping with patience=10. What does patience=10 mean?
options: Train exactly 1000 epochs
Stop training if validation loss doesn't improve for 10 consecutive epochs; prevents overfitting without arbitrary epoch limits
Patience has no effect
Stop after 10 epochs
explain: Early stopping patience=10 halts training if best validation loss doesn't improve for 10 epochs, balancing training duration and generalization.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model for real-time fraud detection. The inference latency is 80ms, but SLA requires <50ms. What options exist?
options: Accept the latency violation
Optimize model (quantization, pruning), upgrade instance type, or cache predictions to meet latency SLA
Disable monitoring
Latency cannot be improved
explain: Model optimization and instance upgrades reduce latency; caching can serve repeated requests without computation.

q: A deployed model's decision thresholds are: predict positive if P(positive) > 0.5. An audit reveals that this threshold results in unequal false positive rates across demographic groups. What is the solution?
options: Keep threshold at 0.5
Demographic groups should not be considered
Use fairness-aware threshold selection: choose threshold that equalized false positive rate or false negative rate across groups
Thresholds cannot be adjusted
explain: Fairness-aware threshold selection balances model utility with fairness constraints across protected attributes.

q: An ML engineer works with categorical features that have hundreds of unique values. One-hot encoding creates a sparse matrix. What is a more efficient approach?
options: Sparse matrices are always best
Remove all categorical features
Use embedding layers (for neural networks) or label encoding with tree models; target encoding encodes by target mean
Use all one-hot encoded features
explain: Embedding and target encoding reduce dimensionality compared to one-hot encoding for high-cardinality features.

q: An ML engineer trains a model using stochastic gradient descent (SGD) with a fixed learning rate of 0.01. Training plateaus at 80% accuracy after 100 epochs and doesn't improve. What should be tried?
options: Use learning rate decay or reduce learning rate; try different optimizers (Adam) that adapt learning rate
Continue training for 1000 epochs
Stop immediately
Increase learning rate
explain: Learning rate decay and adaptive optimizers help escape plateaus and improve convergence to better minima.

q: An ML team deploys models to multiple regions for global availability. Models must be kept in sync across regions. How should this be managed?
options: Manually deploy to each region
Regional sync is not possible
Deploy to only one region
Register models in central Model Registry; replicate across regions using CloudFormation or Terraform
explain: Central Model Registry with cross-region replication ensures consistent model versions across all regions.

q: A lending model is monitored and shows: new loan approval rate 45%, historical approval rate 30%. No retraining occurred. What could explain this approval rate change?
options: Model parameters changed automatically
Model accuracy improved
This is impossible without retraining
Prediction distribution shifted (higher predicted scores); decision threshold remained fixed; or business rule changed approval criteria
explain: Approval rate depends on prediction distribution and threshold; either can shift without model retraining.

q: An ML engineer extracts time-based features from transaction timestamps: hour, day of week, is_weekend. Some customers transact 24/7, others only business hours. What feature engineering step is important?
options: Extract customer-specific patterns: customer's peak transaction hour, typical day of week; personalize features by customer
Ignore customer behavior
All customers have same patterns
Use raw timestamps
explain: Customer-specific temporal patterns are more predictive than global time features; personalization improves model performance.

q: An ML engineer trains a classification model and observes: training loss = 0.1, validation loss = 0.3. The model shows signs of overfitting. What regularization technique is most likely to help?
options: Add L2 regularization, dropout, or data augmentation to reduce model complexity and improve generalization
Increase model size
Remove all regularization
Regularization harms accuracy
explain: L2 regularization and dropout reduce effective model capacity, combating overfitting indicated by high validation loss.

q: An ML engineer manages SageMaker endpoints across different environments (dev, staging, prod). What strategy ensures consistency?
options: Manual management per environment
Different models per environment
Register models in Model Registry; use environment-specific deployment configs (instance types, scaling) from single model version
Consistency is not possible
explain: Model Registry enables single source of truth; environment-specific configs allow consistent model across different tiers.

q: A deployed model for recommendation is monitored. Metrics show high precision but low recall. Many good recommendations are missed. What is the issue?
options: Recall cannot be improved without retraining
Precision and recall are independent
Low recall means the model is conservative; consider lowering decision threshold to recommend more items and improve recall
Increase precision further
explain: Recall improves by lowering threshold; precision-recall tradeoff means lower threshold increases recall at cost of precision.

q: An ML engineer works with sequential event data (user actions: view, click, purchase, return). To train a model predicting the next action, what sequence length should be used?
options: Use fixed window (e.g., last 10 events) as features; balance recency with sufficient context for predictions
Use only the most recent event
Sequence length doesn't matter
Use all historical events (years of data) per user
explain: Fixed sequence windows balance computational efficiency with enough context; very long sequences provide diminishing returns.

q: An ML engineer trains a model on balanced data (50% positive, 50% negative). The model achieves 90% accuracy. The engineer deploys it to production where true data is 90% negative, 10% positive. Will performance generalize?
options: No; the model was trained on different class distribution; accuracy will degrade; use class weights or rebalance training data
Deploy without concern
Accuracy is independent of class distribution
Yes, accuracy will remain 90%
explain: Model trained on balanced data overfits to balanced distribution; deployment on imbalanced data causes performance degradation.

q: An ML engineer exports a PyTorch model to ONNX for inference on CPU. The ONNX model runs 50% slower than expected. What could be the issue?
options: CPU inference lacks GPU optimizations; check ONNX operator support; consider using optimized runtime (ORT)
Export failed silently
Slowness is unavoidable
ONNX is always faster
explain: ONNX Runtime can optimize ONNX models for CPU; some operators may have suboptimal CPU implementations.

q: A model is monitored and shows 99% accuracy on test data but 60% accuracy on production data from a different source. What is the most likely issue?
options: Significant distribution shift between test and production data; model learned patterns specific to test distribution
This is normal
Production data is corrupted
Test data is unrealistic
explain: Large test-production gap indicates distribution shift; production data follows different distribution than test set.

q: An ML engineer prepares data for a time-series model. The raw data has hourly frequency, but business questions are at daily granularity. Should the engineer resample?
options: Resample to daily frequency (aggregate hourly data); match granularity to business questions and reduce noise
Keep hourly frequency
Upsample to minute frequency
Frequency doesn't matter
explain: Resampling to business-relevant frequency reduces noise and aligns features with prediction granularity.

q: An ML engineer uses gradient boosting and observes that adding more trees beyond 100 stops improving validation accuracy. What does this suggest?
options: Model has converged; adding trees causes overfitting; use early stopping to prevent overfitting
Tree count doesn't affect accuracy
Delete earlier trees
Always use many more trees
explain: Gradient boosting convergence plateaus; continuing to add trees risks overfitting without accuracy improvement.

q: An ML engineer deploys an endpoint with auto-scaling enabled. During low traffic periods, instances scale down. A request arrives and the endpoint has zero instances. What happens?
options: Request is immediately served
Auto-scaling prevents this
Request waits for instance launch (cold start); adds latency; configure minimum capacity if cold starts are unacceptable
Request fails instantly
explain: Zero-capacity cold starts add latency; minimum capacity configuration ensures instant availability at cost of idle resources.

q: A model is monitored for fairness using SageMaker Clarify. The bias analysis shows: disparate impact ratio 0.5 (0.5x approval rate for Group B vs Group A). Is this acceptable?
options: Disparate impact is always acceptable
No; standard fairness threshold is 0.8 (80% rule); 0.5 indicates discrimination; retrain with fairness constraints
Bias cannot be measured
Yes, 0.5 ratio is acceptable
explain: 80% rule: protected group should have 80%+ relative outcome rate; 0.5 ratio indicates significant disparity requiring mitigation.

q: An ML engineer builds a training set from web scraping. Some data is sensitive (PII, copyrighted content). Before training, what must be done?
options: Use data as-is
Only use public data
Review data for PII and copyrighted content; redact PII and respect copyright; ensure ethical data practices
Ignore ethical concerns
explain: Responsible ML requires data governance: PII redaction, copyright respect, and ethical sourcing before training.

q: An ML engineer trains a model with batch normalization and sees training loss = 0.05, validation loss = 0.5. What is the issue?
options: Severe overfitting; validation loss 10x training loss indicates model memorizes training data
This is normal
Batch norm is working correctly
Batch norm caused overfitting
explain: Massive training-validation gap is extreme overfitting; regularization (dropout, L2) and data augmentation are needed.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model using multi-model endpoints where multiple models share instance capacity. When should this be preferred over dedicated endpoints?
options: Multi-model is not supported
When model traffic is low and intermittent; shared instances increase utilization and reduce costs
Never use multi-model
Always use multi-model
explain: Multi-model endpoints are cost-efficient for low-traffic or intermittent models; shared capacity reduces overall cost.

q: A fraud model's recall decreases (fewer frauds caught) without any model changes or retraining. What could explain this?
options: Recall changes randomly
Model degraded automatically
Fraud patterns evolved (concept drift); previous features no longer detect fraud; retraining on recent data needed
Recall is constant without retraining
explain: Concept drift causes performance changes without model changes; fraud tactics evolve requiring periodic retraining.

q: An organization ingests customer support transcripts (unstructured text) for a classifier. The texts are very long (5000+ words). What is a practical approach?
options: Text classification needs short inputs
Use entire transcript as-is
Truncate to first 512 tokens, or summarize; extract key sections (problem, resolution) as features
Use only the first sentence
explain: Truncation, summarization, or key section extraction reduces long text to manageable size for training.

q: An ML engineer trains a neural network with 5 hidden layers. Training is very slow. What could speed up training?
options: Remove all hidden layers
Use batch normalization in hidden layers to stabilize training and enable higher learning rates
Train on CPU
Depth doesn't affect training speed
explain: Batch normalization stabilizes deep networks, enabling faster convergence compared to non-normalized deep networks.

q: An ML engineer manages SageMaker endpoints that serve different models for different business lines (finance, retail, healthcare). What governance structure ensures quality?
options: No governance
Governance is unnecessary
Each line manages independently
Centralized Model Registry with approval workflows; each business line has deployment checklists and SLAs
explain: Centralized governance with business-line-specific SLAs ensures accountability and quality across diverse models.

q: A deployed model's explanation (SHAP values) shows that feature X is the top driver for some predictions. However, using feature X directly is illegal. What should be done?
options: Ignore the explanation
Explanations don't inform feature selection
Continue using feature X
Remove feature X or add fairness constraints; consider if X is a proxy for protected attributes
explain: Explanations reveal problematic features (legal, ethical risks); removing illegal or discriminatory features preserves model utility.

q: An ML engineer creates a feature for time-series forecasting: 'day of year' (1-365). This feature is numeric but cyclical (day 1 is close to day 365). Should encoding change?
options: Remove day-of-year feature
Use sin/cos encoding to preserve cyclicity: sin(2π*day/365), cos(2π*day/365)
Cyclicity doesn't matter
Use day of year directly
explain: Sin/cos encoding preserves cyclical structure; linear day values incorrectly suggest day 365 is far from day 1.

q: An ML engineer trains a recommendation model that predicts items a user will like. The model is evaluated on a test set and achieves high precision (90%) but low recall (10%). What is the issue?
options: Precision-recall tradeoff doesn't apply
Precision is the only metric that matters
Recall cannot be improved
Low recall means the model recommends very few items; users miss many good recommendations; increase recommendation count to improve recall
explain: Recommendation coverage depends on recall; low recall misses opportunities; adjusting recommendation threshold improves recall.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model in a regulated industry (healthcare, finance) where audit trails are required. What should be logged?
options: Only model name
Input features, model version, prediction, prediction confidence, timestamp, user ID for auditing decisions
No logging needed
Logging is optional
explain: Audit trails with full prediction context enable compliance verification and investigation of adverse outcomes.

q: A model monitors its own predictions and notices the prediction distribution has shifted significantly. However, true labels haven't arrived yet (prediction lag). Should the team retrain?
options: Wait for true labels to confirm drift through performance metrics; use prediction drift as early signal, but validate before retraining
Never use prediction distribution
Drift cannot be detected without labels
Retrain immediately
explain: Prediction drift is an early signal but requires label confirmation before committing to expensive retraining.

q: An ML engineer processes geographic data (latitude, longitude) for a location-based recommendation model. Should raw coordinates be used as features?
options: Coordinates are always best
No; encode as distances to landmarks, grid cells, or embeddings; raw coordinates are high-dimensional and non-informative
Geographic data is unsuitable for ML
Yes, use coordinates directly
explain: Geographic embeddings or grid cells capture spatial structure better than raw coordinates.

q: An ML engineer trains a model using k-fold cross-validation with k=10. One fold shows accuracy 95%, but other folds show 70%. What does this suggest?
options: Average accuracy is the truth
High variance across folds indicates data heterogeneity or overfitting to specific fold; investigate data issues
Model is robust
Fold variance doesn't matter
explain: High fold variance indicates the model's performance depends heavily on specific data subsets; robustness requires low variance.

q: An ML team uses Kubernetes to manage containerized model services. How should SageMaker models be integrated with Kubernetes?
options: Kubernetes cannot run models
SageMaker is incompatible with Kubernetes
Use SageMaker endpoints only
Export model to Docker container; deploy to Kubernetes; use SageMaker for training and export workflow
explain: SageMaker models can be exported to Docker containers and deployed on Kubernetes for container-orchestrated inference.

q: A model's performance degrades but the business team says: 'We intentionally changed the approval threshold to approve more cases.' The team didn't retrain. Is this acceptable?
options: No monitoring is needed
No; threshold changes alter the precision-recall tradeoff; monitor false positive/negative rates and evaluate downstream impact
Threshold changes are invisible to users
Yes, threshold changes don't affect predictions
explain: Business rule changes (threshold adjustments) affect metrics; impact must be monitored to ensure acceptable tradeoffs.

q: An ML engineer works with image data where different sources have different resolutions and color spaces (RGB, grayscale). Preprocessing is critical before training. What is required?
options: Preprocessing is optional
Convert all images to same color space and resolution; apply normalization (e.g., divide by 255 for 8-bit images)
Use images as-is
Remove images with different specs
explain: Standardized color space and resolution enable efficient batching; normalization stabilizes neural network training.

q: An ML engineer implements a custom training loop in PyTorch instead of using Keras/TensorFlow high-level API. What is a key consideration?
options: Ensure proper gradient computation, backward pass, optimization step, and learning rate scheduling; test on small dataset first
Custom loops are always unnecessary
Custom loops have no impact on results
Debugging is impossible with custom loops
explain: Custom training loops require careful implementation to avoid gradient issues, weight updates, or optimization bugs.

q: An ML engineer deploys a model to production and later discovers it makes predictions in <10ms but the client-side latency is 200ms. Where is the bottleneck?
options: Model is slow
Infrastructure is broken
Network latency, serialization, deserialization, or client-side processing adds overhead; profile to identify bottleneck
Model optimization is the only solution
explain: End-to-end latency includes network and processing overhead; profiling identifies which component dominates latency.

q: A model is monitored for performance. Weekly metrics show: Week 1 accuracy 85%, Week 2 accuracy 84%, Week 3 accuracy 86%. Is the model degrading?
options: No; small fluctuations (±1%) are normal variance; monitor for sustained drift over multiple weeks, not single-week dips
Yes, Week 2 shows degradation
Always retrain on any accuracy change
Accuracy is static without retraining
explain: Week-to-week variance is expected; sustained degradation over multiple weeks indicates real drift requiring investigation.

q: An ML engineer extracts features from a recommendation system: user age, item price, and user-item interaction count. Age and price have very different ranges (age: 18-90, price: $1-$10,000). Should they be normalized before training?
options: Normalize only price
Range differences don't affect models
No normalization needed
Yes; apply feature scaling (StandardScaler or MinMaxScaler) so features contribute equally to distance-based models
explain: Features with large range differences dominate distance-based models (KNN, SVM); normalization ensures fair contribution.

q: An ML engineer trains a model with AUC-ROC = 0.95 (excellent discrimination) but precision-recall AUC = 0.45 (poor). What explains this discrepancy?
options: One metric is wrong
Highly imbalanced data: AUC-ROC is invariant to class distribution; PR-AUC is sensitive to imbalance; focus on PR-AUC
Metrics are inconsistent
Both metrics agree
explain: On imbalanced data, AUC-ROC is misleadingly high; PR-AUC better reflects minority class performance.

q: An ML engineer needs to deploy a model that requires real-time access to large reference data (1GB lookup table). Should this be stored in the model or separately?
options: Embed lookup table in model file
Store lookup separately (DynamoDB, ElastiCache, S3); load at inference time; model contains only scoring code
Lookups are not possible
Duplicate in each model copy
explain: Large lookups should be stored separately to avoid bloating model files and enable fast updates.

q: A model is deployed for customer churn prediction. To ensure explainability, SageMaker Clarify is used. For a prediction 'Customer has 80% churn probability,' what should the explanation show?
options: Top features driving high churn probability (e.g., 'Long period without purchase', 'Increased support tickets'); feature contribution scores
Model architecture
Only the probability
Explanations cannot show feature importance
explain: SHAP explanations show feature contributions to individual predictions, enabling stakeholders to understand model decisions.

q: An ML engineer builds an NLP model and notices that the same phrase appears with different capitalizations ('The Model', 'the model', 'THE MODEL'). Should these be treated as identical?
options: No, preserve original capitalization
Yes; convert all text to lowercase before tokenization to treat variations as identical tokens
Capitalization has no effect
Use case-sensitive tokenization only
explain: Lowercasing normalizes text; different capitalizations of the same word should be treated identically in NLP tasks.

q: An ML engineer trains an ensemble of 5 models (bagging). Individual models have accuracy 75%, but ensemble achieves 85%. Why does ensemble improve?
options: Individual model accuracy is irrelevant
Ensemble has no effect
Ensemble averaging reduces variance from uncorrelated model errors; majority voting on diverse models improves robustness
Ensemble always combines predictions equally
explain: Ensemble methods reduce variance by averaging independent models; diversity in errors maximizes variance reduction benefit.

q: An ML engineer manages SageMaker endpoints and wants to reduce costs without sacrificing availability. What option is available?
options: Use only large instances
Costs cannot be reduced
Keep all instances running always
Use auto-scaling and spot instances; trade slight latency variance for significant cost savings
explain: Auto-scaling removes idle capacity; spot instances reduce compute costs, accepting occasional interruptions and latency variance.

q: A model is monitored and prediction confidence scores (softmax probabilities) have a mean of 0.51 across all predictions. Is the model well-calibrated?
options: No; average probability 0.51 suggests overconfidence; well-calibrated models have probability matching empirical frequencies
Yes, 0.51 is close to 0.5
Calibration is not measurable
All probabilities are equally valid
explain: Calibration: if model predicts 70% probability, outcomes should be positive 70% of time; mean probability 0.51 suggests poor calibration.

q: An ML engineer works with transaction data and needs to encode categorical feature 'country' with 195 unique values. What approach minimizes dimensionality?
options: One-hot encode all 195 categories
Group rare countries as 'Other' to reduce categories to ~50; use target encoding or embedding layers
High cardinality features cannot be used
Remove country entirely
explain: Grouping rare categories and target encoding handle high-cardinality features efficiently without curse of dimensionality.

q: An ML engineer trains a seq2seq model (encoder-decoder) for machine translation. The encoder processes input and produces a context vector. How can encoder output be leveraged for other tasks?
options: Transfer learning doesn't apply to seq2seq
Transfer learning: use pre-trained encoder to generate embeddings for other language tasks (classification, clustering)
Encoder cannot be reused
Encoder output is specific to translation
explain: Pre-trained encoders learn useful language representations; these embeddings transfer to downstream NLP tasks.

q: An ML engineer manages models deployed on SageMaker. A critical bug is discovered in all models simultaneously. How can impact be mitigated?
options: Bugs cannot be rolled back
Use versioning and quick rollback: revert all endpoints to previous model version in minutes
Revert all models at once manually
Stop all predictions
explain: Model versioning and quick rollback capabilities minimize incident impact; practicing rollback procedures is essential.

q: A model is monitored for drift using CloudWatch and SageMaker Model Monitor. An alert triggers: 'Data drift detected.' The team asks: 'Should we retrain immediately?' What is the answer?
options: Drift is always bad
Ignore drift alerts
Yes, always retrain immediately
No; investigate first: is drift from new patterns (retrain) or data quality issues (fix pipeline)?
explain: Data drift requires investigation before action; addressing root cause (quality issues) may be preferable to retraining.

q: An ML engineer creates a dataset by combining customer records from two sources: DB A (clean, recent) and DB B (noisy, older). How should these be combined?
options: Combining sources causes bias
Use only DB B for volume
Remove DB B to ensure quality
Weight DB A samples higher in training; use DB B to expand data; validate that mixing doesn't hurt performance
explain: Data source weighting balances quality and quantity; validation ensures that combining sources doesn't introduce unwanted bias.

q: An ML engineer trains a model using stochastic gradient descent but switches to using the full batch gradient (all data at once per update) hoping to improve stability. Training becomes slower. Why?
options: Gradient type doesn't affect speed
Full batch is always better
Full batch has more stable gradients but requires storing gradients for all data; slower iteration; smaller batches better for wall-clock efficiency
SGD is always faster
explain: Full-batch gradient is stable but computationally expensive; mini-batch balances stability and iteration speed for practical training.

q: An ML engineer uses SageMaker Pipelines to automate model deployment. A pipeline step that evaluates the model sometimes takes 30 minutes, sometimes 2 hours. What is the issue?
options: Evaluation time is always inconsistent
Throughput cannot be improved
Instance type may be insufficient or variable performance; monitor resource usage; consider larger instance types
Evaluation is inherently random
explain: Variable performance indicates resource bottleneck; scaling up instance type or improving evaluation code can stabilize latency.

q: A model deployed for fraud detection has SLA: detect 95% of fraud (recall >= 95%). Currently achieving 92% recall. The business wants improvements. What is the priority action?
options: Reduce the SLA
Analyze false negatives (missed frauds); retrain with fraud-detection-optimized objective; lower decision threshold to increase recall
Recall cannot be improved further
Improve precision
explain: Missing frauds (false negatives) is costly; retraining with recall-focused objectives and threshold adjustment improve fraud detection.

q: An ML engineer prepares training data and discovers that 30% of target labels are assigned by low-confidence human annotators. How should this be handled?
options: Remove all low-confidence labels
Confidence doesn't affect training
Weight low-confidence labels lower in loss function; consider retraining with high-confidence labels only to assess impact
Use all labels without concern
explain: Low-confidence labels may introduce noise; weighting or filtering can improve training signal quality.

q: An ML engineer trains a model that learns to make predictions but the prediction distribution is very skewed: 99% of predictions are class A, 1% class B. The classes are actually balanced (50/50). What went wrong?
options: Model learned the training distribution incorrectly; likely class imbalance in training data or missing class-balancing mechanisms
Prediction distribution matches real data
The model is correct
Models always predict majority class
explain: Skewed predictions despite balanced true distribution indicate training data imbalance or lack of class weighting.

q: An ML team uses AWS Lambda to invoke SageMaker endpoints. Lambda has 15-minute timeout. Model inference takes 2 minutes per request. A batch of 100 requests arrives. What architecture change is needed?
options: Use asynchronous architecture: Lambda writes requests to SQS/S3; separate worker processes inferences; Lambda polls for results
Increase Lambda timeout to 200 minutes
Process sequentially in Lambda
This is impossible
explain: Asynchronous patterns decouple request submission from inference execution, enabling processing of large batches beyond Lambda timeouts.

q: A model's false positive rate has increased from 2% to 8% without any model changes. The input distribution looks identical. What is likely happening?
options: Data drift is the only cause
False positives increase randomly
Model degradation is unexplainable
Target distribution shifted; concept drift causes same inputs to have different outcomes; retrain on recent data
explain: Concept drift (changed input-output relationship) with unchanged input distribution causes performance changes; retraining is needed.

q: An ML engineer extracts features from natural language text using TF-IDF (term frequency-inverse document frequency). A new document arrives with a rare word not in the training vocabulary. How is this handled?
options: The model fails with new words
TF-IDF automatically handles new words
All words are known
Rare words not in training vocabulary are ignored or assigned zero TF-IDF; consider using subword tokenization (BPE) to handle OOV
explain: Out-of-vocabulary (OOV) words are not in training and get zero feature value; subword tokenization handles rare words.

q: An ML engineer trains two models: Model A with 100 parameters, Model B with 1,000,000 parameters. Both achieve 80% training accuracy, but Model B achieves 79% validation accuracy while Model A achieves 78%. Which is better?
options: Model B despite complexity; validation performance is higher; monitor for overfitting, but higher validation accuracy suggests better generalization
Model A is always better
Both are equally good
Parameter count determines quality
explain: Despite complexity, Model B's higher validation accuracy indicates better true performance; larger models can improve generalization if properly trained.

q: An ML engineer uses SageMaker Pipelines for continuous training. The pipeline trains, evaluates, and conditionally deploys based on accuracy threshold. Deployment has not occurred in 2 weeks. What should the team investigate?
options: Pipelines run independently
Deployments are optional
Force deployment anyway
Check pipeline logs for failures; investigate if accuracy is consistently below threshold or if pipeline has errors
explain: Lack of deployment indicates either performance consistently below threshold (investigation needed) or pipeline failures to diagnose.

q: A model's explainability is monitored using SageMaker Clarify. One day, SHAP values show that a previously important feature 'customer_age' has become unimportant. What could explain this?
options: Input distribution shifted; if 'age' is now constant (all same value), it provides no discrimination; investigate data quality or distribution change
SHAP values are random
This is always expected
Feature importance is constant
explain: Feature importance changes indicate distribution shifts; constant features provide no predictive power and warrant investigation.
